title: Generalized non-exponential Gaussian splatting
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables
summary: In this work we generalize 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) to a wider family of physically-based alpha-blending operators. 3DGS has become the standard de-facto for radiance field rendering and reconstruction, given its flexibility and efficiency. At its core, it is based on alpha-blending sorted semitransparent primitives, which in the limit converges to the classic radiative transfer function with exponential transmittance. Inspired by recent research on non-exponential radiative transfer, we generalize the image formation model of 3DGS to non-exponential regimes. Based on this generalization, we use a quadratic transmittance to define sub-linear, linear, and super-linear versions of 3DGS, which exhibit faster-than-exponential decay. We demonstrate that these new non-exponential variants achieve similar quality than the original 3DGS but significantly reduce the number of overdraws, which result on speed-ups of up to $4\times$ in complex real-world captures, on a ray-tracing-based renderer.

AI.tldr: This paper generalizes 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) to non-exponential radiative transfer functions using quadratic transmittance, creating sub-linear, linear, and super-linear variants that reduce overdraws and achieve up to 4x speed-ups while maintaining similar rendering quality.
AI.motivation: 3DGS has become the standard for radiance field rendering due to its flexibility and efficiency. Its core relies on alpha-blending sorted semitransparent primitives with exponential transmittance (converging to classic radiative transfer). Inspired by recent research on non-exponential radiative transfer, the authors seek to generalize 3DGS's image formation model to non-exponential regimes.
AI.method: The authors generalize 3DGS's image formation model using a quadratic transmittance function to define three non-exponential variants: sub-linear (faster-than-exponential decay), linear, and super-linear versions, all exhibiting faster-than-exponential decay compared to the original exponential transmittance.
AI.result: The new non-exponential variants achieve rendering quality comparable to the original 3DGS but significantly reduce overdraws, resulting in up to 4× speed-ups in complex real-world captures on a ray-tracing-based renderer.
AI.conclusion: Non-exponential generalizations of 3DGS provide an efficient alternative that maintains rendering quality while dramatically improving performance through reduced overdraws, demonstrating that moving beyond exponential transmittance offers meaningful computational benefits.

title: Quadratic-Order Geodesics on Meshes
summary: We introduce a novel representation and optimization framework for discrete geodesics on triangle meshes that reduces artifacts of linear methods on uneven and coarse discretizations. Our method computes squared geodesic distances from point and curve sources using piecewise-quadratic elements, exactly reproducing flat distances regardless of mesh quality while improving accuracy over existing approaches on curved meshes. The formulation naturally supports sources placed anywhere on the mesh, not just at vertices.

AI.tldr: A novel framework for computing discrete geodesic distances on triangle meshes using piecewise-quadratic elements that reduces artifacts from linear methods and improves accuracy, supporting arbitrary source locations.
AI.motivation: Linear methods for discrete geodesic computation on triangle meshes produce artifacts on uneven and coarse discretizations, particularly on curved meshes where accuracy is limited. Existing approaches often require sources only at vertices, restricting flexibility.
AI.method: Introduces a representation and optimization framework using piecewise-quadratic elements to compute squared geodesic distances. The formulation supports sources placed anywhere on the mesh, not just at vertices, and exactly reproduces flat distances regardless of mesh quality.
AI.result: The method reduces artifacts of linear methods on uneven and coarse discretizations while improving accuracy over existing approaches on curved meshes. It exactly reproduces flat distances regardless of mesh quality.
AI.conclusion: The piecewise-quadratic element formulation provides a more accurate and robust approach to computing discrete geodesic distances on triangle meshes, with the flexibility to handle sources at arbitrary locations.

title: Goal-Oriented Semantic Communication for ISAC-Enabled Robotic Obstacle Avoidance
comment: 13 pages, 15 figures
summary: We investigate an integrated sensing and communication (ISAC)-enabled BS for the unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) obstacle avoidance task, and propose a goal-oriented semantic communication (GOSC) framework for the BS to transmit sensing and command and control (C&C) signals efficiently and effectively. Our GOSC framework establishes a closed loop for sensing-C&C generation-sensing and C&C transmission: For sensing, a Kalman filter (KF) is applied to continuously predict UAV positions, mitigating the reliance of UAV position acquisition on continuous sensing signal transmission, and enhancing position estimation accuracy through sensing-prediction fusion. Based on the refined estimation position provided by the KF, we develop a Mahalanobis distance-based dynamic window approach (MD-DWA) to generate precise C&C signals under uncertainty, in which we derive the mathematical expression of the minimum Mahalanobis distance required to guarantee collision avoidance. Finally, for efficient sensing and C&C signal transmission, we propose an effectiveness-aware deep Q-network (E-DQN) to determine the transmission of sensing and C&C signals based on their value of information (VoI). The VoI of sensing signals is quantified by the reduction in uncertainty entropy of UAV's position estimation, while the VoI of C&C signals is measured by their contribution to UAV navigation improvement. Extensive simulations validate the effectiveness of our proposed GOSC framework. Compared to the conventional ISAC transmission framework that transmits sensing and C&C signals at every time slot, GOSC achieves the same 100% task success rate while reducing the number of transmitted sensing and C&C signals by 92.4% and the number of transmission time slots by 85.5%.

AI.tldr: An ISAC-enabled BS with a goal-oriented semantic communication (GOSC) framework for UAV obstacle avoidance, combining Kalman filter prediction, Mahalanobis distance-based dynamic window approach, and effectiveness-aware deep Q-network to reduce signal transmission by 85.5% while maintaining 100% task success rate.
AI.motivation: To efficiently and effectively transmit sensing and command and control (C&C) signals for UAV obstacle avoidance using an integrated sensing and communication (ISAC)-enabled base station, addressing the trade-off between sensing accuracy and transmission efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes a GOSC framework with three key components: 1) Kalman filter (KF) for continuous UAV position prediction to reduce reliance on continuous sensing transmission; 2) Mahalanobis distance-based dynamic window approach (MD-DWA) to generate precise C&C signals under uncertainty; 3) Effectiveness-aware deep Q-network (E-DQN) to determine signal transmission based on value of information (VoI), where sensing VoI is quantified by reduction in position estimation entropy and C&C VoI is measured by contribution to navigation improvement.
AI.result: Extensive simulations demonstrate that GOSC achieves the same 100% task success rate as conventional ISAC transmission while reducing transmitted sensing and C&C signals by 92.4% and transmission time slots by 85.5%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GOSC framework effectively balances sensing accuracy and transmission efficiency for UAV obstacle avoidance, demonstrating significant performance improvement over conventional ISAC transmission approaches.

title: The Alignment Flywheel: A Governance-Centric Hybrid MAS for Architecture-Agnostic Safety
summary: Multi-agent systems provide mature methodologies for role decomposition, coordination, and normative governance, capabilities that remain essential as increasingly powerful autonomous decision components are embedded within agent-based systems. While learned and generative models substantially expand system capability, their safety behavior is often entangled with training, making it opaque, difficult to audit, and costly to update after deployment. This paper formalizes the Alignment Flywheel as a governance-centric hybrid MAS architecture that decouples decision generation from safety governance. A Proposer, representing any autonomous decision component, generates candidate trajectories, while a Safety Oracle returns raw safety signals through a stable interface. An enforcement layer applies explicit risk policy at runtime, and a governance MAS supervises the Oracle through auditing, uncertainty-driven verification, and versioned refinement. The central engineering principle is patch locality: many newly observed safety failures can be mitigated by updating the governed oracle artifact and its release pipeline rather than retracting or retraining the underlying decision component. The architecture is implementation-agnostic with respect to both the Proposer and the Safety Oracle, and specifies the roles, artifacts, protocols, and release semantics needed for runtime gating, audit intake, signed patching, and staged rollout across distributed deployments. The result is a hybrid MAS engineering framework for integrating highly capable but fallible autonomous systems under explicit, version-controlled, and auditable oversight.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Alignment Flywheel, a hybrid multi-agent system architecture that decouples decision generation from safety governance, enabling maintainable and auditable AI systems through patch locality principles.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent systems provide essential role decomposition, coordination, and normative governance capabilities. However, learned and generative models expand system capability while making safety behavior opaque, difficult to audit, and costly to update after deployment. The paper addresses the challenge of integrating highly capable but fallible autonomous systems under explicit oversight.
AI.method: The paper formalizes the Alignment Flywheel as a governance-centric hybrid MAS architecture comprising: (1) a Proposer that generates candidate trajectories (any autonomous decision component), (2) a Safety Oracle returning raw safety signals through a stable interface, (3) an enforcement layer applying explicit risk policies at runtime, and (4) a governance MAS supervising the Oracle through auditing, uncertainty-driven verification, and versioned refinement. The architecture is implementation-agnostic and specifies roles, artifacts, protocols, and release semantics for runtime gating, audit intake, signed patching, and staged rollout across distributed deployments.
AI.result: The Alignment Flywheel architecture provides a hybrid MAS engineering framework for integrating highly capable but fallible autonomous systems under explicit, version-controlled, and auditable oversight, with patch locality as a central engineering principle enabling safety failures to be mitigated by updating governed oracle artifacts and release pipelines rather than retraining underlying decision components.
AI.conclusion: By decoupling decision generation from safety governance, the Alignment Flywheel enables more maintainable and auditable AI systems. The patch locality principle allows many newly observed safety failures to be addressed through targeted oracle updates and release pipeline modifications, avoiding costly full retraining while maintaining rigorous oversight across distributed deployments.

title: Safe Whole-Body Loco-Manipulation via Combined Model and Learning-based Control
comment: Accepted to IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA), June 2026, in Vienna, Austria
summary: Simultaneous locomotion and manipulation enables robots to interact with their environment beyond the constraints of a fixed base. However, coordinating legged locomotion with arm manipulation, while considering safety and compliance during contact interaction remains challenging. To this end, we propose a whole-body controller that combines a model-based admittance control for the manipulator arm with a Reinforcement Learning (RL) policy for legged locomotion. The admittance controller maps external wrenches--such as those applied by a human during physical interaction--into desired end-effector velocities, allowing for compliant behavior. The velocities are tracked jointly by the arm and leg controllers, enabling a unified 6-DoF force response. The model-based design permits accurate force control and safety guarantees via a Reference Governor (RG), while robustness is further improved by a Kalman filter enhanced with neural networks for reliable base velocity estimation. We validate our approach in both simulation and hardware using the Unitree Go2 quadruped robot with a 6-DoF arm and wrist-mounted 6-DoF Force/Torque sensor. Results demonstrate accurate tracking of interaction-driven velocities, compliant behavior, and safe, reliable performance in dynamic settings.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a whole-body controller for legged robots that combines model-based admittance control for arm manipulation with reinforcement learning for legged locomotion, enabling safe and compliant human-robot interaction.
AI.motivation: Simultaneous locomotion and manipulation is challenging due to the difficulty of coordinating legged locomotion with arm manipulation while ensuring safety and compliance during contact interaction.
AI.method: A whole-body controller that integrates model-based admittance control for the manipulator arm with an RL policy for legged locomotion. The admittance controller maps external wrenches to desired end-effector velocities. A Reference Governor (RG) provides safety guarantees, while a Kalman filter enhanced with neural networks improves base velocity estimation.
AI.result: Validated in simulation and hardware using the Unitree Go2 quadruped with a 6-DoF arm and wrist-mounted 6-DoF Force/Torque sensor. Results demonstrate accurate tracking of interaction-driven velocities, compliant behavior, and safe, reliable performance in dynamic settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully enables accurate velocity tracking, compliant interaction behavior, and safe, reliable performance in dynamic environments for simultaneous legged locomotion and arm manipulation.

title: StitchCUDA: An Automated Multi-Agents End-to-End GPU Programing Framework with Rubric-based Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary:
Modern machine learning (ML) workloads increasingly rely on GPUs, yet achieving high end-to-end performance remains challenging due to dependencies on both GPU kernel efficiency and host-side settings. Although LLM-based methods show promise on automated GPU kernel generation, prior works mainly focus on single-kernel optimization and do not extend to end-to-end programs, hindering practical deployment.
  To address the challenge, in this work, we propose StitchCUDA, a multi-agent framework for end-to-end GPU program generation, with three specialized agents: a Planner to orchestrate whole system design, a Coder dedicated to implementing it step-by-step, and a Verifier for correctness check and performance profiling using Nsys/NCU. To fundamentally improve the Coder's ability in end-to-end GPU programming, StitchCUDA integrates rubric-based agentic reinforcement learning over two atomic skills, task-to-code generation and feedback-driven code optimization, with combined rubric reward and rule-based reward from real executions. Therefore, the Coder learns how to implement advanced CUDA programming techniques (e.g., custom kernel fusion, cublas epilogue), and we also effectively prevent Coder's reward hacking (e.g., just copy PyTorch code or hardcoding output) during benchmarking. Experiments on KernelBench show that StitchCUDA achieves nearly 100% success rate on end-to-end GPU programming tasks, with 1.72x better speedup over the multi-agent baseline and 2.73x than the RL model baselines.

AI.tldr: StitchCUDA is a multi-agent framework for end-to-end GPU program generation that uses specialized agents (Planner, Coder, Verifier) and reinforcement learning to achieve nearly 100% success rate on GPU programming tasks.
AI.motivation: Modern ML workloads rely on GPUs, but achieving high end-to-end performance is challenging due to dependencies on GPU kernel efficiency and host-side settings. Existing LLM-based methods mainly focus on single-kernel optimization and do not extend to end-to-end programs, hindering practical deployment.
AI.method: StitchCUDA features three specialized agents: a Planner for orchestrating whole system design, a Coder for step-by-step implementation using rubric-based agentic reinforcement learning over task-to-code generation and feedback-driven code optimization, and a Verifier for correctness check and performance profiling using Nsys/NCU. The Coder learns advanced CUDA programming techniques (e.g., custom kernel fusion, cublas epilogue) with combined rubric reward and rule-based reward from real executions.
AI.result: Experiments on KernelBench show StitchCUDA achieves nearly 100% success rate on end-to-end GPU programming tasks, with 1.72x speedup over the multi-agent baseline and 2.73x speedup over RL model baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively enables the Coder to implement advanced CUDA programming techniques while preventing reward hacking during benchmarking, demonstrating that multi-agent orchestration combined with reinforcement learning is effective for end-to-end GPU program generation.

title: Learning Therapist Policy from Therapist-Exoskeleton-Patient Interaction
comment: Accepted at IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: Post-stroke rehabilitation is often necessary for patients to regain proper walking gait. However, the typical therapy process can be exhausting and physically demanding for therapists, potentially reducing therapy intensity, duration, and consistency over time. We propose a Patient-Therapist Force Field (PTFF) to visualize therapist responses to patient kinematics and a Synthetic Therapist (ST) machine learning model to support the therapist in dyadic robot-mediated physical interaction therapy. The first encodes patient and therapist stride kinematics into a shared low-dimensional latent manifold using a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) and models their interaction through a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM), which learns a probabilistic vector field mapping patient latent states to therapist responses. This representation visualizes patient-therapist interaction dynamics to inform therapy strategies and robot controller design. The latter is implemented as a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network trained on patient-therapist interaction data to predict therapist-applied joint torques from patient kinematics. Trained and validated using leave-one-out cross-validation across eight post-stroke patients, the model was integrated into a ROS-based exoskeleton controller to generate real-time torque assistance based on predicted therapist responses. Offline results and preliminary testing indicate the potential of their use as an alternative approach to post-stroke exoskeleton therapy. The PTFF provides understanding of the therapist's actions while the ST frees the human therapist from the exoskeleton, allowing them to continuously monitor the patient's nuanced condition.

AI.tldr: A combined Patient-Therapist Force Field (PTFF) visualization tool and Synthetic Therapist (ST) machine learning model are proposed to support therapist-patient interaction during exoskeleton-assisted post-stroke rehabilitation. The PTFF encodes patient and therapist kinematics into a shared latent manifold using a VAE and models interaction dynamics with a GMM, while the ST LSTM network predicts therapist joint torques from patient kinematics to enable continuous monitoring without exoskeleton burden.
AI.motivation: Therapist fatigue during post-stroke rehabilitation reduces therapy intensity, duration, and consistency. A system is needed to support therapist-patient interaction while freeing therapists from exoskeleton burden.
AI.method: The PTFF uses a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to encode patient and therapist stride kinematics into a shared low-dimensional latent manifold, with a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) modeling interaction as a probabilistic vector field mapping patient latent states to therapist responses. The Synthetic Therapist is an LSTM network trained on interaction data to predict therapist-applied joint torques from patient kinematics, integrated into a ROS-based exoskeleton controller for real-time torque assistance.
AI.result: Trained via leave-one-out cross-validation across eight post-stroke patients, the model demonstrated potential in offline results and preliminary testing. The PTFF provides understanding of therapist actions, while the ST frees human therapists from exoskeleton control, allowing continuous monitoring of patients' nuanced conditions.
AI.conclusion: This combined approach shows promise as an alternative to traditional post-stroke exoskeleton therapy, offering both therapeutic insight through visualization and practical benefits by reducing therapist physical burden.

title: Generalized Per-Agent Advantage Estimation for Multi-Agent Policy Optimization
comment: Accepted at the 25th International Conference on Autonomous Agents and Multiagent Systems (AAMAS 2026)
summary: In this paper, we propose a novel framework for multi-agent reinforcement learning that enhances sample efficiency and coordination through accurate per-agent advantage estimation. The core of our approach is Generalized Per-Agent Advantage Estimator (GPAE), which employs a per-agent value iteration operator to compute precise per-agent advantages. This operator enables stable off-policy learning by indirectly estimating values via action probabilities, eliminating the need for direct Q-function estimation. To further refine estimation, we introduce a double-truncated importance sampling ratio scheme. This scheme improves credit assignment for off-policy trajectories by balancing sensitivity to the agent's own policy changes with robustness to non-stationarity from other agents. Experiments on benchmarks demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing approaches, excelling in coordination and sample efficiency for complex scenarios.

AI.tldr: A novel multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that enhances sample efficiency and coordination through the Generalized Per-Agent Advantage Estimator (GPAE) and a double-truncated importance sampling ratio scheme.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent RL faces challenges in sample efficiency and coordination, particularly with off-policy learning and credit assignment across multiple agents. Existing methods struggle with non-stationarity and policy changes, requiring more precise advantage estimation for effective learning.
AI.method: The core contribution is GPAE, a per-agent value iteration operator that computes precise advantages without direct Q-function estimation by using action probabilities. Additionally, a double-truncated importance sampling ratio scheme is introduced to balance sensitivity to policy changes with robustness to non-stationarity, improving credit assignment for off-policy trajectories.
AI.result: Experiments on benchmark tasks demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms existing methods, showing superior performance in both coordination and sample efficiency, particularly in complex multi-agent scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The combination of GPAE and double-truncated importance sampling provides a robust solution for multi-agent RL challenges, offering significant improvements in learning efficiency and inter-agent coordination compared to state-of-the-art approaches.

title: A Novel Modular Cable-Driven Soft Robotic Arm with Multi-Segment Reconfigurability
comment: 6 pages, 8 figures, Submitted to IEEE/ASME International Conference on Advanced Intelligent Mechatronics
summary: This paper presents a novel, modular, cable-driven soft robotic arm featuring multi-segment reconfigurability. The proposed architecture enables a stackable system with independent segment control, allowing scalable adaptation to diverse structural and application requirements. The system is fabricated from soft silicone material and incorporates embedded tendon-routing channels with a protective dual-helical tendon structure. Experimental results showed that modular stacking substantially expanded the reachable workspace: relative to the single-segment arm, the three-segment configuration achieved up to a 13-fold increase in planar workspace area and a 38.9-fold increase in workspace volume. Furthermore, this study investigated the effect of silicone stiffness on actuator performance. The results revealed a clear trade-off between compliance and stiffness: softer silicone improved bending flexibility, while stiffer silicone improved structural rigidity and load-bearing stability. These results highlight the potential of stiffness tuning to balance compliance and strength for configuring scalable, reconfigurable soft robotic arms.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a novel modular cable-driven soft robotic arm with multi-segment reconfigurability, demonstrating significant workspace expansion through modular stacking and investigating stiffness tuning trade-offs between compliance and structural rigidity.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to develop a modular, cable-driven soft robotic arm with multi-segment reconfigurability that can adapt to diverse structural and application requirements through a stackable system with independent segment control.
AI.method: The system is fabricated from soft silicone material with embedded tendon-routing channels and a protective dual-helical tendon structure. The researchers created modular segments that can be stacked and tested single-segment versus three-segment configurations, investigating the effect of silicone stiffness on actuator performance.
AI.result: Modular stacking substantially expanded the reachable workspace: the three-segment configuration achieved up to a 13-fold increase in planar workspace area and a 38.9-fold increase in workspace volume compared to the single-segment arm. Softer silicone improved bending flexibility, while stiffer silicone improved structural rigidity and load-bearing stability.
AI.conclusion: The results highlight the potential of stiffness tuning to balance compliance and strength for configuring scalable, reconfigurable soft robotic arms.

title: Generative adversarial imitation learning for robot swarms: Learning from human demonstrations and trained policies
comment: Accepted for publication at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: In imitation learning, robots are supposed to learn from demonstrations of the desired behavior. Most of the work in imitation learning for swarm robotics provides the demonstrations as rollouts of an existing policy. In this work, we provide a framework based on generative adversarial imitation learning that aims to learn collective behaviors from human demonstrations. Our framework is evaluated across six different missions, learning both from manual demonstrations and demonstrations derived from a PPO-trained policy. Results show that the imitation learning process is able to learn qualitatively meaningful behaviors that perform similarly well as the provided demonstrations. Additionally, we deploy the learned policies on a swarm of TurtleBot 4 robots in real-robot experiments. The exhibited behaviors preserved their visually recognizable character and their performance is comparable to the one achieved in simulation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a GAIL-based framework for swarm robotics to learn collective behaviors from human demonstrations, achieving qualitatively meaningful behaviors that perform comparably to demonstrations both in simulation and on real TurtleBot 4 robots.
AI.motivation: Most existing work in swarm robotics imitation learning provides demonstrations as rollouts from an existing policy, rather than from human demonstrations.
AI.method: A framework based on generative adversarial imitation learning (GAIL) is proposed to learn collective behaviors from human demonstrations. The framework is evaluated across six different missions, learning from both manual demonstrations and demonstrations derived from a PPO-trained policy.
AI.result: The imitation learning process successfully learns qualitatively meaningful behaviors that perform similarly well as the provided demonstrations. Deployed on real TurtleBot 4 robots, the exhibited behaviors preserved their visually recognizable character and performance is comparable to simulation results.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GAIL-based framework is effective for learning collective behaviors from human demonstrations in swarm robotics applications.

title: COLREGs Compliant Collision Avoidance and Grounding Prevention for Autonomous Marine Navigation
summary: Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships (MASS) are increasingly regarded as a promising solution to address crew shortages, improve navigational safety, and improve operational efficiency in the maritime industry. Nevertheless, the reliable deployment of MASS in real-world environments remains a significant challenge, particularly in congested waters where the majority of maritime accidents occur. This emphasizes the need for safe and regulation-aware motion planning strategies for MASS that are capable of operating under dynamic maritime conditions. This paper presents a unified motion planning method for MASS that achieves real time collision avoidance, compliance with International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs), and grounding prevention. The proposed work introduces a convex optimization method that integrates velocity obstacle-based (VO) collision constraints, COLREGs-based directional constraints, and bathymetry-based grounding constraints to generate computationally efficient, rule-compliant optimal velocity selection. To enhance robustness, the classical VO method is extended to consider uncertainty in the position and velocity estimates of the target vessel. Unnavigable shallow water regions obtained from bathymetric data, which are inherently nonconvex, are approximated via convex geometries using a integer linear programming (ILP), allowing grounding constraints to be incorporated into the motion planning. The resulting optimization generates optimal and dynamically feasible input velocities that meet collision avoidance, regulatory compliance, kinodynamic limits, and grounding prevention requirements. Simulation results involving multi-vessel encounters demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in producing safe and regulation-compliant maneuvers, highlighting the suitability of the proposed approach for real time autonomous maritime navigation.

AI.tldr: A unified convex optimization method for MASS that achieves real-time collision avoidance, COLREGs compliance, and grounding prevention by integrating velocity obstacles, regulatory constraints, and bathymetry-based constraints with uncertainty-aware planning.
AI.motivation: MASS offers solutions for crew shortages and operational efficiency but faces deployment challenges in congested waters where most accidents occur, necessitating safe, regulation-aware motion planning strategies for dynamic maritime conditions.
AI.method: Convex optimization integrating velocity obstacle-based collision constraints, COLREGs-based directional constraints, and bathymetry-based grounding constraints; extended classical VO for target uncertainty; approximated nonconvex shallow water regions using convex geometries via integer linear programming (ILP).
AI.result: Simulation results with multi-vessel encounters demonstrate the method produces safe and regulation-compliant maneuvers that meet collision avoidance, regulatory compliance, kinodynamic limits, and grounding prevention requirements.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach is suitable for real-time autonomous maritime navigation, effectively addressing collision avoidance, COLREGs compliance, and grounding prevention in dynamic environments.

title: A Robust Simulation Framework for Verification and Validation of Autonomous Maritime Navigation in Adverse Weather and Constrained Environments
summary: Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships (MASS) have emerged as a promising solution to enhance navigational safety, operational efficiency, and long-term cost effectiveness. However, their reliable deployment requires rigorous verification and validation (V\&V) under various environmental conditions, including extreme and safety-critical scenarios. This paper presents an enhanced virtual simulation framework to support the V\&V of MASS in realistic maritime environments, with particular emphasis on the influence of weather and bathymetry on autonomous navigation performance. The framework incorporates a high-fidelity environmental modeling suite capable of simulating adverse weather conditions such as rain, fog, and wave dynamics. The key factors that affect weather, such as rain and visibility, are parameterized to affect sea-state characteristics, perception, and sensing systems, resulting in position and velocity uncertainty, reduced visibility, and degraded situational awareness. Furthermore, high-resolution bathymetric data from major U.S. ports are integrated to enable depth-aware navigation, grounding prevention capabilities, and evaluation of vessel controllability in shallow or confined waterways. The proposed framework offers extensive configurability, enabling systematic testing in a wide spectrum of maritime conditions, including scenarios that are impractical or unsafe to replicate in real-world trials, thus supporting the V\&V of MASS.

AI.tldr: A high-fidelity virtual simulation framework for verifying and validating Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships (MASS) that incorporates realistic weather modeling (rain, fog, waves) and high-resolution bathymetric data to test performance in extreme and safety-critical maritime environments.
AI.motivation: Massive deployment of Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships (MASS) requires rigorous verification and validation (V&V) under various environmental conditions, particularly extreme and safety-critical scenarios that are difficult or unsafe to test in real-world trials.
AI.method: An enhanced virtual simulation framework that includes: (1) a high-fidelity environmental modeling suite for rain, fog, and wave dynamics; (2) parameterized weather factors affecting sea-state characteristics, perception, and sensing systems; (3) high-resolution bathymetric data integration from major U.S. ports; and (4) extensive configurability for systematic testing across diverse maritime conditions.
AI.result: The framework enables testing of MASS in scenarios that are impractical or unsafe to replicate in real-world trials, providing position and velocity uncertainty, reduced visibility, and degraded situational awareness in adverse weather, while supporting depth-aware navigation and grounding prevention in shallow waterways.
AI.conclusion: The proposed simulation framework offers a comprehensive solution for MASS V&V by enabling systematic testing under realistic environmental conditions, including extreme weather and challenging bathymetry, thus supporting the reliable deployment of autonomous maritime vessels.

title: Instant and Reversible Adhesive-free Bonding Between Silicones and Glossy Papers for Soft Robotics
summary: Integrating silicone with non-extensible materials is a common strategy used in the fabrication of fluidically-driven soft actuators, yet conventional approaches often rely on irreversible adhesives or embedding processes that are labor-intensive and difficult to modify. This work presents silicone-glossy paper bonding (SGB), a rapid, adhesive-free, and solvent-reversible bonding approach that forms robust silicone-paper interfaces simply through contact. The SGB interface withstands high mechanical loads (shear strength > 61 kPa) and can be fully detached and reassembled via ethanol immersion without loss of performance, enabling component reuse and rapid redesign. Characterization studies indicate that surface functional groups primarily govern adhesion on the glossy paper and the modulus of the silicone, while durability and environmental response clarify the conditions for reversible debonding. The results further suggest a synergistic interaction of hydrogen bonding and oligomer diffusion, yielding strong yet reconfigurable adhesion. Soft actuators fabricated using SGB design exhibit equal or greater performance compared to conventional embedded-layer design and enable programmable actuation modes, including contraction, bending, and twisting. By simplifying fabrication while supporting reuse and rapid iteration, SGB offers a scalable and sustainable platform for rapid prototyping in soft robotics.

AI.tldr: Silicone-glossy paper bonding (SGB) is a rapid, adhesive-free, solvent-reversible bonding approach that forms robust silicone-paper interfaces through simple contact, enabling component reuse and rapid redesign for soft robotics.
AI.motivation: Conventional approaches for integrating silicone with non-extensible materials rely on irreversible adhesives or labor-intensive embedding processes that are difficult to modify.
AI.method: A rapid, adhesive-free, and solvent-reversible bonding approach called silicone-glossy paper bonding (SGB) that forms robust silicone-paper interfaces simply through contact.
AI.result: The SGB interface withstands high mechanical loads (shear strength ≥61 kPa) and can be fully detached and reassembled via ethanol immersion without loss of performance. Soft actuators fabricated using SGB exhibit equal or greater performance compared to conventional embedded-layer designs.
AI.conclusion: By simplifying fabrication while supporting reuse and rapid iteration, SGB offers a scalable and sustainable platform for rapid prototyping in soft robotics.

title: Learning Object-Centric Spatial Reasoning for Sequential Manipulation in Cluttered Environments
summary: Robotic manipulation in cluttered environments presents a critical challenge for automation. Recent large-scale, end-to-end models demonstrate impressive capabilities but often lack the data efficiency and modularity required for retrieving objects in dense clutter. In this work, we argue for a paradigm of specialized, decoupled systems and present Unveiler, a framework that explicitly separates high-level spatial reasoning from low-level action execution. Unveiler's core is a lightweight, transformer-based Spatial Relationship Encoder (SRE) that sequentially identifies the most critical obstacle for removal. This discrete decision is then passed to a rotation-invariant Action Decoder for execution. We demonstrate that this decoupled architecture is not only more computationally efficient in terms of parameter count and inference time, but also significantly outperforms both classic end-to-end policies and modern, large-model-based baselines in retrieving targets from dense clutter. The SRE is trained in two stages: imitation learning from heuristic demonstrations provides sample-efficient initialization, after which PPO fine-tuning enables the policy to discover removal strategies that surpass the heuristic in dense clutter. Our results, achieving up to 97.6\% success in partially occluded and 90.0\% in fully occluded scenarios in simulation, make a case for the power of specialized, object-centric reasoning in complex manipulation tasks. Additionally, we demonstrate that the SRE's spatial reasoning transfers zero-shot to real scenes, and validate the full system on a physical robot requiring only geometric workspace calibration; no learned components are retrained.

AI.tldr: Unveiler is a decoupled robotic manipulation framework that separates high-level spatial reasoning (Spatial Relationship Encoder) from low-level action execution (Action Decoder), achieving 97.6% and 90.0% success rates in partially and fully occluded clutter scenarios respectively, with zero-shot transfer to real robots.
AI.motivation: While large-scale end-to-end models show impressive robotic manipulation capabilities, they lack the data efficiency and modularity needed for retrieving objects in dense clutter. The authors argue for specialized, decoupled systems that explicitly separate spatial reasoning from action execution.
AI.method: Unveiler consists of a lightweight transformer-based Spatial Relationship Encoder (SRE) that identifies the most critical obstacle for removal, and a rotation-invariant Action Decoder for execution. Training is two-stage: imitation learning from heuristic demonstrations provides sample-efficient initialization, followed by PPO fine-tuning to discover superior removal strategies in dense clutter.
AI.result: The decoupled architecture outperforms both classic end-to-end policies and modern large-model baselines. Results: 97.6% success in partially occluded scenarios and 90.0% in fully occluded scenarios in simulation. The SRE transfers zero-shot to real scenes, with full system validated on physical robot requiring only geometric workspace calibration (no learned components retrained).
AI.conclusion: Specialized, object-centric reasoning architectures are more effective than monolithic end-to-end approaches for complex manipulation tasks in cluttered environments, offering better computational efficiency, data efficiency, and real-world transferability.

title: PathSpace: Rapid continuous map approximation for efficient SLAM using B-Splines in constrained environments
summary:
Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) plays a crucial role in enabling autonomous vehicles to navigate previously unknown environments.
  Semantic SLAM mostly extends visual SLAM, leveraging the higher density information available to reason about the environment in a more human-like manner. This allows for better decision making by exploiting prior structural knowledge of the environment, usually in the form of labels. Current semantic SLAM techniques still mostly rely on a dense geometric representation of the environment, limiting their ability to apply constraints based on context. We propose PathSpace, a novel semantic SLAM framework that uses continuous B-splines to represent the environment in a compact manner, while also maintaining and reasoning through the continuous probability density functions required for probabilistic reasoning. This system applies the multiple strengths of B-splines in the context of SLAM to interpolate and fit otherwise discrete sparse environments. We test this framework in the context of autonomous racing, where we exploit pre-specified track characteristics to produce significantly reduced representations at comparable levels of accuracy to traditional landmark based methods and demonstrate its potential in limiting the resources used by a system with minimal accuracy loss.

AI.tldr: PathSpace is a novel semantic SLAM framework that uses continuous B-splines to represent environments compactly while maintaining continuous probability density functions for probabilistic reasoning, enabling reduced representations with minimal accuracy loss in autonomous racing applications.
AI.motivation: Current semantic SLAM techniques rely on dense geometric representations, which limits their ability to apply context-based constraints. The authors seek to leverage B-spline strengths for more efficient representation of environments in SLAM systems.
AI.method: PathSpace employs continuous B-splines to represent the environment in a compact manner while maintaining and reasoning through continuous probability density functions for probabilistic reasoning. The system applies B-spline strengths in SLAM context to interpolate and fit discrete sparse environments.
AI.result: The framework was tested in autonomous racing, exploiting pre-specified track characteristics. It produces significantly reduced representations at comparable accuracy levels to traditional landmark-based methods, demonstrating its potential for resource-efficient systems with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: PathSpace successfully demonstrates that continuous B-spline representations can achieve comparable accuracy to traditional methods while significantly reducing the computational resources required, making it promising for resource-constrained autonomous navigation systems.

title: Give me scissors: Collision-Free Dual-Arm Surgical Assistive Robot for Instrument Delivery
comment: 8 pages, 10 figures. Accepted by IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA), 2026
summary: During surgery, scrub nurses are required to frequently deliver surgical instruments to surgeons, which can lead to physical fatigue and decreased focus. Robotic scrub nurses provide a promising solution that can replace repetitive tasks and enhance efficiency. Existing research on robotic scrub nurses relies on predefined paths for instrument delivery, which limits their generalizability and poses safety risks in dynamic environments. To address these challenges, we present a collision-free dual-arm surgical assistive robot capable of performing instrument delivery. A vision-language model is utilized to automatically generate the robot's grasping and delivery trajectories in a zero-shot manner based on surgeons' instructions. A real-time obstacle minimum distance perception method is proposed and integrated into a unified quadratic programming framework. This framework ensures reactive obstacle avoidance and self-collision prevention during the dual-arm robot's autonomous movement in dynamic environments. Extensive experimental validations demonstrate that the proposed robotic system achieves an 83.33% success rate in surgical instrument delivery while maintaining smooth, collision-free movement throughout all trials. The project page and source code are available at https://give-me-scissors.github.io/.

AI.tldr: A dual-arm surgical assistive robot that uses vision-language models for zero-shot trajectory generation and quadratic programming for real-time obstacle avoidance, achieving 83.33% success rate in instrument delivery.
AI.motivation: Surgical scrub nurses experience physical fatigue and decreased focus during repetitive instrument delivery tasks. Existing robotic scrub nurse systems rely on predefined paths, limiting generalizability and posing safety risks in dynamic surgical environments.
AI.method: A collision-free dual-arm robot equipped with a vision-language model for zero-shot grasping and delivery trajectory generation from surgeon instructions. A real-time obstacle minimum distance perception method is integrated into a unified quadratic programming framework for reactive obstacle avoidance and self-collision prevention.
AI.result: The robotic system achieves an 83.33% success rate in surgical instrument delivery while maintaining smooth, collision-free movement throughout all trials.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system effectively addresses the limitations of existing robotic scrub nurses by combining zero-shot trajectory generation with reactive obstacle avoidance, demonstrating high success rates and safety in dynamic surgical environments. The integration of vision-language models and quadratic programming enables autonomous, safe instrument delivery that enhances surgical efficiency.

title: Tensegrity Robot Endcap-Ground Contact Estimation with Symmetry-aware Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network
comment: Preprint; 7 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables
summary: Tensegrity robots possess lightweight and resilient structures but present significant challenges for state estimation due to compliant and distributed ground contacts. This paper introduces a symmetry-aware heterogeneous graph neural network (Sym-HGNN) that infers contact states directly from proprioceptive measurements, including IMU and cable-length histories, without dedicated contact sensors. The network incorporates the robot's dihedral symmetry $D_3$ into the message-passing process to enhance sample efficiency and generalization. The predicted contacts are integrated into a state-of-the-art contact-aided invariant extended Kalman filter (InEKF) for improved pose estimation. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves up to 15% higher accuracy and 5% higher F1-score using only 20% of the training data compared to the CNN and MI-HGNN baselines, while maintaining low-drift and physically consistent state estimation results comparable to ground truth contacts. This work highlights the potential of fully proprioceptive sensing for accurate and robust state estimation in tensegrity robots. Code available at: https://github.com/Jonathan-Twz/Tensegrity-Sym-HGNN

AI.tldr: A symmetry-aware heterogeneous graph neural network (Sym-HGNN) for contact state estimation in tensegrity robots using only proprioceptive measurements (IMU and cable-length histories), integrated with an InEKF for improved pose estimation.
AI.motivation: Tensegrity robots possess lightweight and resilient structures but face significant challenges for state estimation due to compliant and distributed ground contacts. Current approaches often require dedicated contact sensors, which adds complexity and cost.
AI.method: The paper introduces Sym-HGNN, which infers contact states directly from proprioceptive measurements without dedicated sensors. The network incorporates the robot's dihedral symmetry (D3) into the message-passing process to enhance sample efficiency and generalization. The predicted contacts are integrated into a contact-aided invariant extended Kalman filter (InEKF) for improved pose estimation.
AI.result: Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves up to 15% higher accuracy and 5% higher F1-score using only 20% of the training data compared to CNN and MI-HGNN baselines, while maintaining low-drift and physically consistent state estimation results.
AI.conclusion: This work highlights the potential of fully proprioceptive sensing for accurate and robust state estimation in tensegrity robots, demonstrating that symmetry-aware architectures can significantly improve sample efficiency and generalization performance.

title: Wukong-Omni: Design, Modeling and Control of a Multi-mode Robot for Air, Land, and Underwater Exploration with All-in-One Propulsion Unit
comment: 19 pages, 27 figures
summary: In flood disaster rescue scenarios, partially submerged buildings prevent aerial robots from accessing lower levels, limiting mission effectiveness. To address this challenge, this paper presents Wukong-Omni, a novel multimode robot capable of operating across land, air, and underwater using a unified propulsion system. The system is enabled by an innovative mechanical design that allows motor reuse and improves thrust generation. Efficiency and peak thrust are enhanced through simulation and tank-based optimization. Experimental results show a 100 percent improvement in propulsion efficiency and a 150 percent increase in maximum thrust compared with direct installation methods. Dynamic models for the three operating domains are developed, and a unified cross-domain control framework is proposed. Comprehensive experiments validate stable locomotion and smooth transition across domains. Outdoor experiments further demonstrate robustness and adaptability in real-world environments.

AI.tldr: Wukong-Omni is a multimode robot capable of land, air, and underwater operation using a unified propulsion system, achieving 100% efficiency improvement and 150% thrust increase through mechanical design and optimization.
AI.motivation: Partially submerged buildings in flood disaster rescue scenarios prevent aerial robots from accessing lower levels, limiting mission effectiveness.
AI.method: A novel mechanical design enables motor reuse and improved thrust generation, with simulation and tank-based optimization to enhance efficiency and peak thrust. A unified cross-domain control framework is developed based on dynamic models for three operating domains.
AI.result: Experimental results show a 100% improvement in propulsion efficiency and a 150% increase in maximum thrust compared to direct installation methods. Stable locomotion and smooth transitions across domains are achieved. Outdoor experiments demonstrate robustness and adaptability in real-world environments.
AI.conclusion: Wukong-Omni successfully enables unified land-air-underwater operation, offering significant performance improvements and demonstrating practical utility in flood rescue scenarios.

title: Uni-Skill: Building Self-Evolving Skill Repository for Generalizable Robotic Manipulation
comment: Accepted to ICRA2026
summary: While skill-centric approaches leverage foundation models to enhance generalization in compositional tasks, they often rely on fixed skill libraries, limiting adaptability to new tasks without manual intervention. To address this, we propose Uni-Skill, a Unified Skill-centric framework that supports skill-aware planning and facilitates automatic skill evolution. Unlike prior methods that restrict planning to predefined skills, Uni-Skill requests for new skill implementations when existing ones are insufficient, ensuring adaptable planning with self-augmented skill library. To support automatic implementation of diverse skills requested by the planning module, we construct SkillFolder, a VerbNet-inspired repository derived from large-scale unstructured robotic videos. SkillFolder introduces a hierarchical skill taxonomy that captures diverse skill descriptions at multiple levels of abstraction. By populating this taxonomy with large-scale, automatically annotated demonstrations, Uni-Skill shifts the paradigm of skill acquisition from inefficient manual annotation to efficient offline structural retrieval. Retrieved examples provide semantic supervision over behavior patterns and fine-grained references for spatial trajectories, enabling few-shot skill inference without deployment-time demonstrations. Comprehensive experiments in both simulation and real-world settings verify the state-of-the-art performance of Uni-Skill over existing VLM-based skill-centric approaches, highlighting its advanced reasoning capabilities and strong zero-shot generalization across a wide range of novel tasks.

AI.tldr: Uni-Skill is a unified skill-centric framework that enables skill-aware planning and automatic skill evolution through SkillFolder—a hierarchical skill taxonomy derived from large-scale robotic videos—shifting skill acquisition from manual annotation to efficient offline structural retrieval for few-shot inference.
AI.motivation: Skill-centric approaches leveraging foundation models for compositional tasks often rely on fixed skill libraries, limiting adaptability to new tasks without manual intervention. The core challenge is the inability to dynamically adapt planning when existing skills are insufficient.
AI.method: Uni-Skill proposes (1) skill-aware planning with automatic skill evolution—requesting new skill implementations when needed, (2) SkillFolder—a VerbNet-inspired hierarchical skill taxonomy built from large-scale unstructured robotic videos with automatically annotated demonstrations, and (3) offline structural retrieval that provides semantic supervision and fine-grained spatial trajectory references for few-shot skill inference without deployment-time demonstrations.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments in both simulation and real-world settings demonstrate state-of-the-art performance over existing VLM-based skill-centric approaches, with advanced reasoning capabilities and strong zero-shot generalization across a wide range of novel tasks.
AI.conclusion: Uni-Skill successfully shifts the paradigm of skill acquisition from inefficient manual annotation to efficient offline structural retrieval, demonstrating that hierarchical skill taxonomies derived from large-scale unstructured data can enable adaptive planning and few-shot skill inference without deployment-time demonstrations.

title: cuNRTO: GPU-Accelerated Nonlinear Robust Trajectory Optimization
summary: Robust trajectory optimization enables autonomous systems to operate safely under uncertainty by computing control policies that satisfy the constraints for all bounded disturbances. However, these problems often lead to large Second Order Conic Programming (SOCP) constraints, which are computationally expensive. In this work, we propose the CUDA Nonlinear Robust Trajectory Optimization (cuNRTO) framework by introducing two dynamic optimization architectures that have direct application to robust decision-making and are implemented on CUDA. The first architecture, NRTO-DR, leverages the Douglas-Rachford (DR) splitting method to solve the SOCP inner subproblems of NRTO, thereby significantly reducing the computational burden through parallel SOCP projections and sparse direct solves. The second architecture, NRTO-FullADMM, is a novel variant that further exploits the problem structure to improve scalability using the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM). Finally, we provide GPU implementation of the proposed methodologies using custom CUDA kernels for SOC projection steps and cuBLAS GEMM chains for feedback gain updates. We validate the performance of cuNRTO through simulated experiments on unicycle, quadcopter, and Franka manipulator models, demonstrating speedup up to 139.6$\times$.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces cuNRTO, a CUDA-based framework for robust trajectory optimization that employs two dynamic optimization architectures (NRTO-DR using Douglas-Rachford splitting and NRTO-FullADMM using ADMM) to solve computationally expensive SOCP constraints efficiently, achieving up to 139.6× speedup on GPU.
AI.motivation: Robust trajectory optimization enables safe autonomous operation under uncertainty but requires solving large Second Order Conic Programming (SOCP) constraints, which are computationally expensive and limit real-time applicability.
AI.method: Two optimization architectures: (1) NRTO-DR using Douglas-Rachford splitting with parallel SOCP projections and sparse direct solves; (2) NRTO-FullADMM exploiting problem structure with ADMM for improved scalability. GPU implementation employs custom CUDA kernels for SOC projection steps and cuBLAS GEMM chains for feedback gain updates.
AI.result: Validated on unicycle, quadcopter, and Franka manipulator models, demonstrating up to 139.6× speedup compared to conventional methods, significantly reducing computational burden.
AI.conclusion: The cuNRTO framework successfully addresses the computational challenges of robust trajectory optimization through GPU-accelerated architectures, making real-time robust decision-making feasible for complex autonomous systems.

title: Compositional Visual Planning via Inference-Time Diffusion Scaling
summary: Diffusion models excel at short-horizon robot planning, yet scaling them to long-horizon tasks remains challenging due to computational constraints and limited training data. Existing compositional approaches stitch together short segments by separately denoising each component and averaging overlapping regions. However, this suffers from instability as the factorization assumption breaks down in noisy data space, leading to inconsistent global plans. We propose that the key to stable compositional generation lies in enforcing boundary agreement on the estimated clean data (Tweedie estimates) rather than on noisy intermediate states. Our method formulates long-horizon planning as inference over a chain-structured factor graph of overlapping video chunks, where pretrained short-horizon video diffusion models provide local priors. At inference time, we enforce boundary agreement through a novel combination of synchronous and asynchronous message passing that operates on Tweedie estimates, producing globally consistent guidance without requiring additional training. Our training-free framework demonstrates significant improvements over existing baselines, effectively generalizing to unseen start-goal combinations that were not present in the original training data. Project website: https://comp-visual-planning.github.io/

AI.tldr: A training-free framework for long-horizon robot planning using Tweedie estimates and message passing on overlapping video chunks, enabling stable compositional generation without additional training.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models excel at short-horizon robot planning but struggle with long-horizon tasks due to computational constraints and limited training data. Existing compositional approaches stitch together short segments but are unstable due to factorization assumptions breaking down in noisy data space.
AI.method: The method formulates long-horizon planning as inference over a chain-structured factor graph of overlapping video chunks. Pretrained short-horizon video diffusion models provide local priors. Boundary agreement is enforced through a novel combination of synchronous and asynchronous message passing that operates on Tweedie estimates rather than noisy intermediate states. The approach is training-free.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates significant improvements over existing baselines and effectively generalizes to unseen start-goal combinations not present in the original training data.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing boundary agreement on Tweedie estimates rather than noisy intermediate states is key to stable compositional generation in long-horizon planning. The proposed training-free framework achieves globally consistent guidance without requiring additional training.

title: Watch Your Step: Learning Semantically-Guided Locomotion in Cluttered Environment
comment: Submitted to IROS 2026
summary: Although legged robots demonstrate impressive mobility on rough terrain, using them safely in cluttered environments remains a challenge. A key issue is their inability to avoid stepping on low-lying objects, such as high-cost small devices or cables on flat ground. This limitation arises from a disconnection between high-level semantic understanding and low-level control, combined with errors in elevation maps during real-world operation. To address this, we introduce SemLoco, a Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework designed to avoid obstacles precisely in densely cluttered environments. SemLoco uses a two-stage RL approach that combines both soft and hard constraints and performs pixel-wise foothold safety inference, enabling more accurate foot placement. Additionally, SemLoco integrates a semantic map to assign traversability costs rather than relying solely on geometric data. SemLoco significantly reduces collisions and improves safety around sensitive objects, enabling reliable navigation in situations where traditional controllers would likely cause damage. Experimental results further demonstrate that SemLoco can be effectively applied to more complex, unstructured real-world environments.

AI.tldr: SemLoco is a Reinforcement Learning framework that enables legged robots to avoid low-lying obstacles in cluttered environments by combining semantic maps with pixel-wise foothold safety inference and a two-stage RL approach using soft and hard constraints.
AI.motivation: Legged robots struggle with safe navigation in cluttered environments because they cannot avoid stepping on low-lying objects (e.g., small devices, cables) on flat ground. This limitation stems from the disconnection between high-level semantic understanding and low-level control, as well as errors in elevation maps during real-world operation.
AI.method: SemLoco employs a two-stage RL approach that combines both soft and hard constraints and performs pixel-wise foothold safety inference for more accurate foot placement. It integrates a semantic map to assign traversability costs rather than relying solely on geometric data.
AI.result: SemLoco significantly reduces collisions and improves safety around sensitive objects, enabling reliable navigation in scenarios where traditional controllers would likely cause damage. It can be effectively applied to complex, unstructured real-world environments.
AI.conclusion: SemLoco bridges the gap between semantic understanding and low-level control for legged robots, demonstrating that semantic information combined with reinforcement learning can enable safe navigation in cluttered, real-world settings where traditional geometric approaches fail.

title: IMR-LLM: Industrial Multi-Robot Task Planning and Program Generation using Large Language Models
summary: In modern industrial production, multiple robots often collaborate to complete complex manufacturing tasks. Large language models (LLMs), with their strong reasoning capabilities, have shown potential in coordinating robots for simple household and manipulation tasks. However, in industrial scenarios, stricter sequential constraints and more complex dependencies within tasks present new challenges for LLMs. To address this, we propose IMR-LLM, a novel LLM-driven Industrial Multi-Robot task planning and program generation framework. Specifically, we utilize LLMs to assist in constructing disjunctive graphs and employ deterministic solving methods to obtain a feasible and efficient high-level task plan. Based on this, we use a process tree to guide LLMs to generate executable low-level programs. Additionally, we create IMR-Bench, a challenging benchmark that encompasses multi-robot industrial tasks across three levels of complexity. Experimental results indicate that our method significantly surpasses existing methods across all evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: IMR-LLM is an LLM-driven framework for industrial multi-robot task planning that uses disjunctive graphs and process trees to generate executable programs, outperforming existing methods on a new IMR-Bench benchmark.
AI.motivation: While LLMs show promise in coordinating robots for simple household tasks, industrial scenarios present stricter sequential constraints and more complex task dependencies that existing methods struggle to handle effectively.
AI.method: IMR-LLM framework: (1) uses LLMs to construct disjunctive graphs; (2) applies deterministic solving to obtain feasible high-level task plans; (3) employs process trees to guide LLMs in generating executable low-level programs. Also introduces IMR-Bench benchmark with three complexity levels of multi-robot industrial tasks.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that IMR-LLM significantly surpasses existing methods across all evaluation metrics on the IMR-Bench benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LLM-driven framework effectively addresses the challenges of industrial multi-robot task planning by combining disjunctive graph construction, deterministic solving, and process tree-guided program generation to produce feasible and efficient task plans.

title: MMH-Planner: Multi-Mode Hybrid Trajectory Planning Method for UAV Efficient Flight Based on Real-Time Spatial Awareness
summary: Motion planning is a critical component of intelligent unmanned systems, enabling their complex autonomous operations. However, current planning algorithms still face limitations in planning efficiency due to inflexible strategies and weak adaptability. To address this, this paper proposes a multi-mode hybrid trajectory planning method for UAVs based on real-time environmental awareness, which dynamically selects the optimal planning model for high-quality trajectory generation in response to environmental changes. First, we introduce a goal-oriented spatial awareness method that rapidly assesses flight safety in the upcoming environments. Second, a multi-mode hybrid trajectory planning mechanism is proposed, which can enhance the planning efficiency by selecting the optimal planning model for trajectory generation based on prior spatial awareness. Finally, we design a lazy replanning strategy that triggers replanning only when necessary to reduce computational resource consumption while maintaining flight quality. To validate the performance of the proposed method, we conducted comprehensive comparative experiments in simulation environments. Results demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) algorithms across multiple metrics, achieving the best performance particularly in terms of the average number of planning iterations and computational cost per iteration. Furthermore, the effectiveness of our approach is further verified through real-world flight experiments integrated with a self-developed intelligent UAV platform.

AI.tldr: A multi-mode hybrid trajectory planning method for UAVs that dynamically selects optimal planning models based on real-time environmental awareness, using goal-oriented spatial assessment, a hybrid planning mechanism, and lazy replanning to improve efficiency and adaptability.
AI.motivation: Current planning algorithms face limitations in planning efficiency due to inflexible strategies and weak adaptability. The need is for a method that can dynamically select optimal planning models in response to environmental changes.
AI.method: 1) Goal-oriented spatial awareness method for rapid flight safety assessment. 2) Multi-mode hybrid trajectory planning mechanism that selects optimal planning models based on prior spatial awareness. 3) Lazy replanning strategy that triggers replanning only when necessary to reduce computational resource consumption.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms existing SOTA algorithms across multiple metrics, particularly achieving the best performance in average number of planning iterations and computational cost per iteration. Real-world flight experiments on a self-developed UAV platform further validated effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The multi-mode hybrid trajectory planning method effectively improves planning efficiency and adaptability for UAVs by dynamically selecting appropriate planning models and optimizing computational resource usage.

title: Robust Tightly-Coupled Filter-Based Monocular Visual-Inertial State Estimation and Graph-Based Evaluation for Autonomous Drone Racing
comment: 8 pages, 9 figures
summary: Autonomous drone racing (ADR) demands state estimation that is simultaneously computationally efficient and resilient to the perceptual degradation experienced during extreme velocity and maneuvers. Traditional frameworks typically rely on conventional visual-inertial pipelines with loosely-coupled gate-based Perspective-n-Points (PnP) corrections that suffer from a rigid requirement for four visible features and information loss in intermediate steps. Furthermore, the absence of GNSS and Motion Capture systems in uninstrumented, competitive racing environments makes the objective evaluation of such systems remarkably difficult. To address these limitations, we propose ADR-VINS, a robust, monocular visual-inertial state estimation framework based on an Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF) tailored for autonomous drone racing. Our approach integrates direct pixel reprojection errors from gate corners features as innovation terms within the filter. By bypassing intermediate PnP solvers, ADR-VINS maintains valid state updates with as few as two visible corners and utilizes robust reweighting instead of RANSAC-based schemes to handle outliers, enhancing computational efficiency. Furthermore, we introduce ADR-FGO, an offline Factor-Graph Optimization framework to generate high-fidelity reference trajectories that facilitate post-flight performance evaluation and analysis on uninstrumented, GNSS-denied environments. The proposed system is validated using TII-RATM dataset, where ADR-VINS achieves an average RMS translation error of 0.134 m, while ADR-FGO yields 0.060 m as a smoothing-based reference. Finally, ADR-VINS was successfully deployed in the A2RL Drone Championship Season 2, maintaining stable and robust estimation despite noisy detections during high-agility flight at top speeds of 20.9 m/s. We further utilize ADR-FGO for post-flight evaluation in uninstrumented racing environments.

AI.tldr: ADR-VINS is a monocular visual-inertial state estimation framework based on ESKF for autonomous drone racing that integrates direct pixel reprojection errors as innovation terms, bypassing intermediate PnP solvers and enabling robust updates with as few as two visible corners. Complementing this is ADR-FGO, an offline Factor-Graph Optimization framework for generating high-fidelity reference trajectories in uninstrumented, GNSS-denied environments for post-flight evaluation.
AI.motivation: Autonomous drone racing demands state estimation that is simultaneously computationally efficient and resilient to perceptual degradation at extreme velocities and maneuvers. Traditional visual-inertial pipelines with loosely-coupled PnP corrections suffer from rigid requirements for four visible features and information loss in intermediate steps. Furthermore, the absence of GNSS and Motion Capture systems in uninstrumented competitive racing environments makes objective evaluation remarkably difficult.
AI.method: ADR-VINS: A robust monocular visual-inertial framework based on an Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF) that integrates direct pixel reprojection errors from gate corners as innovation terms, bypassing intermediate PnP solvers to maintain valid state updates with as few as two visible corners. Uses robust reweighting instead of RANSAC-based schemes for outlier handling. ADR-FGO: An offline Factor-Graph Optimization framework for generating high-fidelity reference trajectories to facilitate post-flight performance evaluation and analysis in uninstrumented, GNSS-denied environments.
AI.result: On the TII-RATM dataset, ADR-VINS achieves an average RMS translation error of 0.134 m, while ADR-FGO yields 0.060 m as a smoothing-based reference. Successfully deployed in A2RL Drone Championship Season 2, maintaining stable and robust estimation despite noisy detections at top speeds of 20.9 m/s.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ADR-VINS framework effectively addresses the computational and perceptual challenges of autonomous drone racing by enabling robust state estimation with minimal visible features and without reliance on GNSS or Motion Capture systems. The accompanying ADR-FGO framework enables accurate post-flight evaluation in uninstrumented racing environments, demonstrating the system's practical deployment capability in real-world competitive scenarios.

title: Agentic Self-Evolutionary Replanning for Embodied Navigation
comment: 8 pages, 10 figures, 4 tables, submitted to IEEE for possible publication
summary: Failure is inevitable for embodied navigation in complex environments. To enhance the resilience, replanning (RP) is a viable option, where the robot is allowed to fail, but is capable of adjusting plan until success. However, existing RP approaches freeze the ego action model and miss the opportunities to explore better plans by upgrading the robot itself. To address this limitation, we propose Self-Evolutionary RePlanning, or SERP for short, which leads to a paradigm shift from frozen models towards evolving models by run-time learning from recent experiences. In contrast to existing model evolution approaches that often get stuck at predefined static parameters, we introduce agentic self-evolving action model that uses in-context learning with auto-differentiation (ILAD) for adaptive function adjustment and global parameter reset. To achieve token-efficient replanning for SERP, we also propose graph chain-of-thought (GCOT) replanning with large language model (LLM) inference over distilled graphs. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that SERP achieves higher success rate with lower token expenditure over various benchmarks, validating its superior robustness and efficiency across diverse environments.

AI.tldr: Self-Evolutionary RePlanning (SERP) enables embodied robots to adaptively evolve their action models during replanning by combining in-context learning with auto-differentiation (ILAD) and graph chain-of-thought (GCOT) replanning with LLM inference, achieving higher success rates with lower token costs across simulation and real-world environments.
AI.motivation: Existing replanning approaches freeze the robot's ego action model, preventing the robot from upgrading itself through run-time learning and missing opportunities to explore better plans when failures occur in complex embodied navigation environments.
AI.method: SERP introduces an agentic self-evolving action model that uses in-context learning with auto-differentiation (ILAD) for adaptive function adjustment and global parameter reset. It also employs graph chain-of-thought (GCOT) replanning with large language model inference over distilled graphs to achieve token-efficient replanning.
AI.result: Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that SERP achieves higher success rates with lower token expenditure compared to existing methods across various benchmarks, validating its superior robustness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: SERP represents a paradigm shift from frozen to evolving models in embodied navigation, successfully combining run-time learning with efficient token usage to achieve robust and efficient navigation in diverse environments.

title: SPARC: Spatial-Aware Path Planning via Attentive Robot Communication
summary: Efficient communication is critical for decentralized Multi-Robot Path Planning (MRPP), yet existing learned communication methods treat all neighboring robots equally regardless of their spatial proximity, leading to diluted attention in congested regions where coordination matters most. We propose Relation enhanced Multi Head Attention (RMHA), a communication mechanism that explicitly embeds pairwise Manhattan distances into the attention weight computation, enabling each robot to dynamically prioritize messages from spatially relevant neighbors. Combined with a distance-constrained attention mask and GRU gated message fusion, RMHA integrates seamlessly with MAPPO for stable end-to-end training. In zero-shot generalization from 8 training robots to 128 test robots on 40x40 grids, RMHA achieves approximately 75 percent success rate at 30 percent obstacle density outperforming the best baseline by over 25 percentage points. Ablation studies confirm that distance-relation encoding is the key contributor to success rate improvement in high-density environments. Index Terms-Multi-robot path planning, graph attention mechanism, multi-head attention, communication optimization, cooperative decision-making

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Relation enhanced Multi Head Attention (RMHA), a communication mechanism for decentralized Multi-Robot Path Planning that incorporates Manhattan distance embeddings into attention computation, enabling robots to dynamically prioritize messages from spatially relevant neighbors.
