RMHA achieves 75% success rate in zero-shot generalization from 8 to 128 robots on 40x40 grids at 30% obstacle density, outperforming baselines by over 25 percentage points.
AI.motivation: Efficient communication is critical for decentralized MRPP, but existing learned communication methods treat all neighboring robots equally regardless of spatial proximity, leading to diluted attention in congested regions where coordination matters most.
AI.method: The authors propose RMHA which explicitly embeds pairwise Manhattan distances into attention weight computation, enabling dynamic prioritization of messages from spatially relevant neighbors. RMHA combines with a distance-constrained attention mask and GRU gated message fusion, integrating seamlessly with MAPPO for stable end-to-end training.
AI.result: In zero-shot generalization from 8 training robots to 128 test robots on 40x40 grids, RMHA achieves approximately 75% success rate at 30% obstacle density, outperforming the best baseline by over 25 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Ablation studies confirm that distance-relation encoding is the key contributor to success rate improvement in high-density environments.

title: Design, Modeling and Direction Control of a Wire-Driven Robotic Fish Based on a 2-DoF Crank-Slider Mechanism
comment: Accepted by ICRA 2026
summary: Robotic fish have attracted growing attention in recent years owing to their biomimetic design and potential applications in environmental monitoring and biological surveys. Among robotic fish employing the Body-Caudal Fin (BCF) locomotion pattern, motor-driven actuation is widely adopted. Some approaches utilize multiple servo motors to achieve precise body curvature control, while others employ a brushless motor to drive the tail via wire or rod, enabling higher oscillation and swimming speeds. However, the former approaches typically result in limited swimming speed, whereas the latter suffer from poor maneuverability, with few capable of smooth turning. To address this trade-off, we develop a wire-driven robotic fish equipped with a 2-degree-of-freedom (DoF) crank-slider mechanism that decouples propulsion from steering, enabling both high swimming speed and agile maneuvering. In this paper, we first present the design of the robotic fish, including the elastic skeleton, waterproof structure, and the actuation mechanism that realizes the decoupling. We then establish the actuation modeling and body dynamics to analyze the locomotion behavior. Furthermore, we propose a combined feedforward-feedback control strategy to achieve independent regulation of propulsion and steering. Finally, we validate the feasibility of the design, modeling, and control through a series of prototype experiments, demonstrating swimming, turning, and directional control.

AI.tldr: A wire-driven robotic fish with a 2-DoF crank-slider mechanism that decouples propulsion from steering, achieving both high swimming speed and agile maneuverability.
AI.motivation: Existing BCF robotic fish face a trade-off: multi-servo approaches have precise curvature control but limited speed, while brushless motor approaches have higher speed but poor maneuverability and smooth turning.
AI.method: Wire-driven robotic fish design with 2-DoF crank-slider mechanism; includes elastic skeleton, waterproof structure, and decoupling actuation mechanism; establishes actuation modeling and body dynamics; proposes combined feedforward-feedback control strategy for independent propulsion and steering regulation.
AI.result: Prototype experiments demonstrate feasibility, showing successful swimming, turning, and directional control capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The 2-DoF crank-slider mechanism effectively decouples propulsion from steering, enabling robotic fish to achieve both high swimming speed and agile maneuverability simultaneously.

title: CoFL: Continuous Flow Fields for Language-Conditioned Navigation
comment: 20 pages, 11 figures
summary: Language-conditioned navigation pipelines often rely on brittle modular components or costly action-sequence generation. To address these limitations, we present CoFL, an end-to-end policy that directly maps a bird's-eye view (BEV) observation and a language instruction to a continuous flow field for navigation. Instead of predicting discrete action tokens or sampling action chunks via iterative denoising, CoFL outputs instantaneous velocities that can be queried at arbitrary 2D projected locations. Trajectories are obtained by numerical integration of the predicted field, producing smooth motion that remains reactive under closed-loop execution. To enable large-scale training, we build a dataset of over 500k BEV image-instruction pairs, each procedurally annotated with a flow field and a trajectory derived from BEV semantic maps built on Matterport3D and ScanNet. By training on a mixed distribution, CoFL significantly outperforms modular Vision-Language Model (VLM)-based planners and generative policy baselines on strictly unseen scenes. Finally, we deploy CoFL zero-shot in real-world experiments with overhead BEV observations across multiple layouts, maintaining reliable closed-loop control and a high success rate.

AI.tldr: CoFL is an end-to-end navigation policy that maps BEV observations and language instructions to continuous flow fields, enabling smooth trajectories via numerical integration without discrete action tokens or costly action-sequence generation.
AI.motivation: Language-conditioned navigation pipelines often rely on brittle modular components or costly action-sequence generation, which are significant limitations that need to be addressed.
AI.method: CoFL directly maps a bird's-eye view (BEV) observation and language instruction to a continuous flow field, outputting instantaneous velocities queryable at arbitrary 2D locations. Trajectories are obtained through numerical integration of the predicted field. The authors built a dataset of over 500k BEV image-instruction pairs with procedurally annotated flow fields and trajectories derived from BEV semantic maps on Matterport3D and ScanNet.
AI.result: CoFL significantly outperforms modular Vision-Language Model (VLM)-based planners and generative policy baselines on strictly unseen scenes. Zero-shot deployment with overhead BEV observations maintains reliable closed-loop control and high success rate across multiple layouts.
AI.conclusion: CoFL provides an effective end-to-end approach to language-conditioned navigation that overcomes the limitations of brittle modular components and costly action-sequence generation, demonstrating superior performance and reliable zero-shot deployment in real-world environments.

title: Rhythm: Learning Interactive Whole-Body Control for Dual Humanoids
summary: Realizing interactive whole-body control for multi-humanoid systems is critical for unlocking complex collaborative capabilities in shared environments. Although recent advancements have significantly enhanced the agility of individual robots, bridging the gap to physically coupled multi-humanoid interaction remains challenging, primarily due to severe kinematic mismatches and complex contact dynamics. To address this, we introduce Rhythm, the first unified framework enabling real-world deployment of dual-humanoid systems for complex, physically plausible interactions. Our framework integrates three core components: (1) an Interaction-Aware Motion Retargeting (IAMR) module that generates feasible humanoid interaction references from human data; (2) an Interaction-Guided Reinforcement Learning (IGRL) policy that masters coupled dynamics via graph-based rewards; and (3) a real-world deployment system that enables robust transfer of dual-humanoid interaction. Extensive experiments on physical Unitree G1 robots demonstrate that our framework achieves robust interactive whole-body control, successfully transferring diverse behaviors such as hugging and dancing from simulation to reality.

AI.tldr: Rhythm is the first unified framework enabling real-world deployment of dual-humanoid systems for complex, physically plausible interactive whole-body control, integrating motion retargeting, graph-based reinforcement learning, and real-world deployment capabilities.
AI.motivation: Interactive whole-body control for multi-humanoid systems is critical for complex collaborative capabilities in shared environments. While recent advancements have significantly enhanced individual robot agility, bridging the gap to physically coupled multi-humanoid interaction remains challenging, primarily due to severe kinematic mismatches and complex contact dynamics.
AI.method: Rhythm integrates three core components: (1) Interaction-Aware Motion Retargeting (IAMR) module that generates feasible humanoid interaction references from human data; (2) Interaction-Guided Reinforcement Learning (IGRL) policy that masters coupled dynamics via graph-based rewards; and (3) a real-world deployment system that enables robust transfer of dual-humanoid interaction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on physical Unitree G1 robots demonstrate that Rhythm achieves robust interactive whole-body control, successfully transferring diverse behaviors such as hugging and dancing from simulation to reality.
AI.conclusion: Rhythm successfully enables the real-world deployment of dual-humanoid systems for complex, physically plausible interactions, overcoming the challenges of kinematic mismatches and contact dynamics that have previously hindered multi-humanoid collaborative capabilities.

title: Emerging trends in Cislunar Space for Lunar Science Exploration and Space Robotics aiding Human Spaceflight Safety
comment: Conference Proceedings of 2nd IAA Conference on AI in and for Space (2nd IAA SPAICE), Suzhou, China, 1-3 November, 2025
summary: In recent years, the Moon has emerged as an unparalleled extraterrestrial testbed for advancing cuttingedge technological and scientific research critical to enabling sustained human presence on its surface and supporting future interplanetary exploration. This study identifies and investigates two pivotal research domains with substantial transformative potential for accelerating humanity interplanetary aspirations. First is Lunar Science Exploration with Artificial Intelligence and Space Robotics which focusses on AI and Space Robotics redefining the frontiers of space exploration. Second being Space Robotics aid in manned spaceflight to the Moon serving as critical assets for pre-deployment infrastructure development, In-Situ Resource Utilization, surface operations support, and astronaut safety assurance. By integrating autonomy, machine learning, and realtime sensor fusion, space robotics not only augment human capabilities but also serve as force multipliers in achieving sustainable lunar exploration, paving the way for future crewed missions to Mars and beyond.

AI.tldr: The Moon serves as an unparalleled testbed for advancing AI and space robotics to enable sustainable human presence and future interplanetary exploration.
AI.motivation: The Moon has emerged as an unparalleled extraterrestrial testbed for advancing cutting-edge technological and scientific research critical to enabling sustained human presence on its surface and supporting future interplanetary exploration.
AI.method: The study identifies and investigates two pivotal research domains: (1) Lunar Science Exploration with AI and Space Robotics, and (2) Space Robotics aid in manned spaceflight to the Moon. The research focuses on how AI and space robotics can augment human capabilities and serve as force multipliers.
AI.result: By integrating autonomy, machine learning, and real-time sensor fusion, space robotics can significantly augment human capabilities and serve as force multipliers in achieving sustainable lunar exploration.
AI.conclusion: Space robotics, when integrated with AI, autonomy, and machine learning, can pave the way for future crewed missions to Mars and beyond.

title: Tracing Back Error Sources to Explain and Mitigate Pose Estimation Failures
summary: Robust estimation of object poses in robotic manipulation is often addressed using foundational general estimators, that aim to handle diverse error sources naively within a single model. Still, they struggle due to environmental uncertainties, while requiring long inference times and heavy computation. In contrast, we propose a modular, uncertainty-aware framework that attributes pose estimation errors to specific error sources and applies targeted mitigation strategies only when necessary. Instantiated with Iterative Closest Point (ICP) as a simple and lightweight pose estimator, we leverage our framework for real-world robotic grasping tasks. By decomposing pose estimation into failure detection, error attribution, and targeted recovery, we significantly improve the robustness of ICP and achieve competitive performance compared to foundation models, while relying on a substantially simpler and faster pose estimator.

AI.tldr: A modular, uncertainty-aware framework that decomposes pose estimation into failure detection, error attribution, and targeted recovery, enabling a simple ICP estimator to achieve robust performance comparable to foundation models.
AI.motivation: Foundational general pose estimators struggle with environmental uncertainties and are computationally heavy; they naively handle diverse error sources in a single model rather than addressing them efficiently.
AI.method: Proposes a modular framework with three components: (1) failure detection, (2) error attribution to identify specific error sources, and (3) targeted recovery with mitigation strategies applied only when needed. Instantiated with Iterative Closest Point (ICP) as the base pose estimator.
AI.result: The framework significantly improves ICP's robustness in real-world robotic grasping tasks, achieving competitive performance compared to foundation models while being substantially simpler and faster.
AI.conclusion: Error attribution and targeted mitigation enable lightweight estimators like ICP to match the robustness of complex foundation models, demonstrating that specialized, modular approaches can outperform monolithic solutions.

title: Self-supervised Domain Adaptation for Visual 3D Pose Estimation of Nano-drone Racing Gates by Enforcing Geometric Consistency
comment: Accepted at ICRA 2026
summary: We consider the task of visually estimating the relative pose of a drone racing gate in front of a nano-quadrotor, using a convolutional neural network pre-trained on simulated data to regress the gate's pose. Due to the sim-to-real gap, the pre-trained model underperforms in the real world and must be adapted to the target domain. We propose an unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) approach using only real image sequences collected by the drone flying an arbitrary trajectory in front of a gate; sequences are annotated in a self-supervised fashion with the drone's odometry as measured by its onboard sensors. On this dataset, a state consistency loss enforces that two images acquired at different times yield pose predictions that are consistent with the drone's odometry. Results indicate that our approach outperforms other SoA UDA approaches, has a low mean absolute error in position (x=26, y=28, z=10 cm) and orientation ($ψ$=13${^{\circ}}$), an improvement of 40% in position and 37% in orientation over a baseline. The approach's effectiveness is appreciable with as few as 10 minutes of real-world flight data and yields models with an inference time of 30.4ms (33 fps) when deployed aboard the Crazyflie 2.1 Brushless nano-drone.

AI.tldr: A CNN for visual drone racing gate pose estimation is adapted from simulation to reality via unsupervised domain adaptation using only 10 minutes of real flight data, achieving 40% better position and 37% better orientation accuracy than baseline with 33 fps inference on nano-quadrotor hardware.
AI.motivation: CNNs pre-trained on simulated data suffer from sim-to-real gap when applied to real drone racing pose estimation tasks, requiring adaptation to the target domain without access to ground truth pose labels.
AI.method: Unsupervised domain adaptation approach using only real image sequences collected by the drone flying arbitrary trajectories; sequences are self-supervised with drone's onboard odometry; a state consistency loss enforces that pose predictions are consistent with the measured odometry.
AI.result: Outperforms other state-of-the-art UDA approaches, achieving mean absolute errors of 26cm (x), 28cm (y), 10cm (z) in position and 13° in orientation (ψ); 40% improvement in position and 37% improvement in orientation over baseline; effective with as few as 10 minutes of real-world flight data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unsupervised domain adaptation method successfully bridges the sim-to-real gap for drone racing gate pose estimation, delivering high accuracy (40% position, 37% orientation improvement) and low latency (33 fps) on real nano-quadrotor hardware, with minimal data requirements.

title: DreamFlow: Local Navigation Beyond Observation via Conditional Flow Matching in the Latent Space
summary: Local navigation in cluttered environments often suffers from dense obstacles and frequent local minima. Conventional local planners rely on heuristics and are prone to failure, while deep reinforcement learning(DRL)based approaches provide adaptability but are constrained by limited onboard sensing. These limitations lead to navigation failures because the robot cannot perceive structures outside its field of view. In this paper, we propose DreamFlow, a DRL-based local navigation framework that extends the robot's perceptual horizon through conditional flow matching(CFM). The proposed CFM based prediction module learns probabilistic mapping between local height map latent representation and broader spatial representation conditioned on navigation context. This enables the navigation policy to predict unobserved environmental features and proactively avoid potential local minima. Experimental results demonstrate that DreamFlow outperforms existing methods in terms of latent prediction accuracy and navigation performance in simulation. The proposed method was further validated in cluttered real world environments with a quadrupedal robot. The project page is available at https://dreamflow-icra.github.io.

AI.tldr: DreamFlow is a DRL-based local navigation framework that extends the robot's perceptual horizon through conditional flow matching, enabling prediction of unobserved environmental features to avoid local minima.
AI.motivation: Local navigation in cluttered environments suffers from dense obstacles and local minima. Conventional planners rely on heuristics and are prone to failure, while DRL approaches are constrained by limited onboard sensing (field of view limitations), preventing robots from perceiving structures outside their direct view, which leads to navigation failures.
AI.method: DreamFlow employs conditional flow matching (CFM) to create a prediction module that learns a probabilistic mapping between local height map latent representations and broader spatial representations, conditioned on navigation context. This enables the navigation policy to predict unobserved environmental features and proactively avoid potential local minima.
AI.result: DreamFlow outperforms existing methods in both latent prediction accuracy and navigation performance in simulation. The method was further validated in cluttered real-world environments using a quadrupedal robot.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CFM-based approach successfully extends perceptual horizon for local navigation, demonstrating superior performance over existing methods in both simulated and real-world cluttered environments with quadruped robots.

title: CASSR: Continuous A-Star Search through Reachability for real time footstep planning
summary: Footstep planning involves a challenging combinatorial search. Traditional A* approaches require discretising reachability constraints, while Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP) supports continuous formulations but quickly becomes intractable, especially when rotations are included. We present CASSR, a novel framework that recursively propagates convex, continuous formulations of a robot's kinematic constraints within an A* search. Combined with a new cost-to-go heuristic based on the EPA algorithm, CASSR efficiently plans contact sequences of up to 30 footsteps in under 125 ms. Experiments on biped locomotion tasks demonstrate that CASSR outperforms traditional discretised A* by up to a factor of 100, while also surpassing a commercial MIP solver. These results show that CASSR enables fast, reliable, and real-time footstep planning for biped robots.

title: MA-CoNav: A Master-Slave Multi-Agent Framework with Hierarchical Collaboration and Dual-Level Reflection for Long-Horizon Embodied VLN
summary: Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) aims to empower robots with the ability to perform long-horizon navigation in unfamiliar environments based on complex linguistic instructions. Its success critically hinges on establishing an efficient ``language-understanding -- visual-perception -- embodied-execution'' closed loop. Existing methods often suffer from perceptual distortion and decision drift in complex, long-distance tasks due to the cognitive overload of a single agent. Inspired by distributed cognition theory, this paper proposes MA-CoNav, a Multi-Agent Collaborative Navigation framework. This framework adopts a ``Master-Slave'' hierarchical agent collaboration architecture, decoupling and distributing the perception, planning, execution, and memory functions required for navigation tasks to specialized agents. Specifically, the Master Agent is responsible for global orchestration, while the Subordinate Agent group collaborates through a clear division of labor: an Observation Agent generates environment descriptions, a Planning Agent performs task decomposition and dynamic verification, an Execution Agent handles simultaneous mapping and action, and a Memory Agent manages structured experiences. Furthermore, the framework introduces a ``Local-Global'' dual-stage reflection mechanism to dynamically optimize the entire navigation pipeline. Empirical experiments were conducted using a real-world indoor dataset collected by a Limo Pro robot, with no scene-specific fine-tuning performed on the models throughout the process. The results demonstrate that MA-CoNav comprehensively outperforms existing mainstream VLN methods across multiple metrics.

AI.tldr: MA-CoNav proposes a Multi-Agent Collaborative Navigation framework with a hierarchical "Master-Slave" architecture, distributing perception, planning, execution, and memory functions across specialized agents (Observation, Planning, Execution, Memory) plus a Master Agent for global orchestration, achieving superior performance on VLN tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing VLN methods suffer from perceptual distortion and decision drift in complex tasks due to cognitive overload of single agents. The paper draws inspiration from distributed cognition theory to address these limitations.
AI.method: MA-CoNav adopts a "Master-Slave" hierarchical agent collaboration architecture: Master Agent for global orchestration, Subordinate Agent group with Observation Agent (environment descriptions), Planning Agent (task decomposition and dynamic verification), Execution Agent (simultaneous mapping and action), and Memory Agent (structured experiences). Additionally introduces a "Local-Global" dual-stage reflection mechanism.
AI.result: Experiments on a real-world indoor dataset collected by a Limo Pro robot (with no scene-specific fine-tuning) demonstrate that MA-CoNav comprehensively outperforms existing mainstream VLN methods across multiple metrics.
AI.conclusion: The distributed cognition approach successfully addresses perceptual distortion and decision drift in VLN by decoupling and distributing cognitive functions across specialized agents, establishing an efficient "language-understanding -- visual-perception -- embodied-execution" closed loop.

title: Architectural HRI: Towards a Robotic Paradigm Shift in Human-Building Interaction
summary: Recent advances in sensing, communication, interfaces, control, and robotics are expanding Human-Building Interaction (HBI) beyond adaptive building services and facades toward the physical actuation of architectural space. In parallel, research in robotic furniture, swarm robotics, and shape-changing spaces shows that architectural elements can now be robotically augmented to move, reconfigure, and adapt space. We propose that these advances promise a paradigm shift in HBI, in which multiple building layers physically adapt in synchrony to support occupant needs and sustainability goals more holistically. Conversely, we argue that this emerging paradigm also provides an ideal case for transferring HRI knowledge to unconventional robotic morphologies, including the interpretation of the robot as multiple architectural layers or even as a building. However, this research agenda remains challenged by the temporal, spatial, and social complexity of architectural HRI, and by fragmented knowledge across HCI, environmental psychology, cognitive science, and architecture. We therefore call for interdisciplinary research that unifies the why, what, and how of robotic actuation in architectural forms.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a paradigm shift in Human-Building Interaction (HBI) where architectural elements become robotically augmented to physically adapt synchronously to occupant needs and sustainability goals, while simultaneously offering a platform for transferring Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) knowledge to unconventional robotic morphologies.
AI.motivation: Recent advances in sensing, communication, interfaces, control, and robotics have enabled architectural elements to move, reconfigure, and adapt space through robotic furniture, swarm robotics, and shape-changing spaces. These advances promise a paradigm shift in HBI where multiple building layers physically adapt in synchrony to support occupant needs and sustainability goals more holistically. Additionally, this emerging paradigm provides an ideal case for transferring HRI knowledge to unconventional robotic morphologies.
AI.method: The authors identify and analyze the challenges facing this research agenda, including the temporal, spatial, and social complexity of architectural HRI, as well as the fragmented knowledge across HCI, environmental psychology, cognitive science, and architecture. They call for interdisciplinary research that unifies the why, what, and how of robotic actuation in architectural forms.
AI.result: The research agenda remains challenged by the complexity of architectural HRI and the fragmented nature of existing knowledge across disciplines. However, the authors argue that the potential paradigm shift in HBI and the opportunity for transferring HRI knowledge to unconventional robotic morphologies provide strong motivation for continued research.
AI.conclusion: The authors call for interdisciplinary research that unifies the why, what, and how of robotic actuation in architectural forms to address the challenges and realize the potential of this emerging paradigm shift in Human-Building Interaction.

title: CMoE: Contrastive Mixture of Experts for Motion Control and Terrain Adaptation of Humanoid Robots
summary: For effective deployment in real-world environments, humanoid robots must autonomously navigate a diverse range of complex terrains with abrupt transitions. While the Vanilla mixture of experts (MoE) framework is theoretically capable of modeling diverse terrain features, in practice, the gating network exhibits nearly uniform expert activations across different terrains, weakening the expert specialization and limiting the model's expressive power. To address this limitation, we introduce CMoE, a novel single-stage reinforcement learning framework that integrates contrastive learning to refine expert activation distributions. By imposing contrastive constraints, CMoE maximizes the consistency of expert activations within the same terrain while minimizing their similarity across different terrains, thereby encouraging experts to specialize in distinct terrain types. We validated our approach on the Unitree G1 humanoid robot through a series of challenging experiments. Results demonstrate that CMoE enables the robot to traverse continuous steps up to 20 cm high and gaps up to 80 cm wide, while achieving robust and natural gait across diverse mixed terrains, surpassing the limits of existing methods. To support further research and foster community development, we release our code publicly.

AI.tldr: CMoE is a contrastive mixture of experts framework for humanoid robot terrain navigation that uses contrastive constraints to enhance expert specialization, enabling traversal of steps up to 20 cm high and gaps up to 80 cm wide.
AI.motivation: Humanoid robots require autonomous navigation across diverse terrains with abrupt transitions. Vanilla MoE frameworks suffer from nearly uniform expert activations across terrains, weakening expert specialization and limiting model expressive power for terrain adaptation.
AI.method: CMoE introduces a single-stage reinforcement learning framework integrating contrastive learning to refine expert activation distributions. It imposes contrastive constraints that maximize expert activation consistency within the same terrain while minimizing similarity across different terrains, encouraging experts to specialize in distinct terrain types.
AI.result: Validated on the Unitree G1 humanoid robot, CMoE enables traversal of continuous steps up to 20 cm high and gaps up to 80 cm wide, achieving robust and natural gait across diverse mixed terrains, outperforming existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The contrastive learning approach successfully addresses the limitation of uniform expert activations in vanilla MoE, enabling superior expert specialization and significantly enhancing humanoid robot capabilities for challenging terrain navigation.

title: RL-Based Coverage Path Planning for Deformable Objects on 3D Surfaces
comment: 8 pages, 8 figures. Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)
summary: Currently, manipulation tasks for deformable objects often focus on activities like folding clothes, handling ropes, and manipulating bags. However, research on contact-rich tasks involving deformable objects remains relatively underdeveloped. When humans use cloth or sponges to wipe surfaces, they rely on both vision and tactile feedback. Yet, current algorithms still face challenges with issues like occlusion, while research on tactile perception for manipulation is still evolving. Tasks such as covering surfaces with deformable objects demand not only perception but also precise robotic manipulation. To address this, we propose a method that leverages efficient and accessible simulators for task execution. Specifically, we train a reinforcement learning agent in a simulator to manipulate deformable objects for surface wiping tasks. We simplify the state representation of object surfaces using harmonic UV mapping, process contact feedback from the simulator on 2D feature maps, and use scaled grouped convolutions (SGCNN) to extract features efficiently. The agent then outputs actions in a reduced-dimensional action space to generate coverage paths. Experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms previous approaches in key metrics, including total path length and coverage area. We deploy these paths on a Kinova Gen3 manipulator to perform wiping experiments on the back of a torso model, validating the feasibility of our approach.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement learning framework for robotic surface wiping with deformable objects using harmonic UV mapping and SGCNN to generate optimal coverage paths.
AI.motivation: Contact-rich manipulation with deformable objects (like wiping surfaces with cloth) remains underdeveloped despite being common for humans. Current algorithms struggle with occlusion and lack effective tactile perception integration, making precise surface covering challenging.
AI.method: Trains an RL agent in simulators to perform surface wiping tasks by: (1) simplifying surface state representation using harmonic UV mapping, (2) processing contact feedback on 2D feature maps, (3) employing Scaled Grouped Convolutions (SGCNN) for efficient feature extraction, and (4) outputting actions in a reduced-dimensional action space to generate coverage paths.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms previous approaches in key metrics: total path length and coverage area. Successfully deployed on a Kinova Gen3 manipulator to wipe the back of a torso model, validating feasibility.
AI.conclusion: The integration of efficient tactile perception processing with harmonic UV mapping and SGCNN enables robots to perform contact-rich deformable object manipulation tasks like surface wiping more effectively than previous methods, demonstrating practical applicability on real hardware.

title: Look Forward to Walk Backward: Efficient Terrain Memory for Backward Locomotion with Forward Vision
comment: Accepted for 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)
summary: Legged robots with egocentric forward-facing depth cameras can couple exteroception and proprioception to achieve robust forward agility on complex terrain. When these robots walk backward, the forward-only field of view provides no preview. Purely proprioceptive controllers can remain stable on moderate ground when moving backward but cannot fully exploit the robot's capabilities on complex terrain and must collide with obstacles. We present Look Forward to Walk Backward (LF2WB), an efficient terrain-memory locomotion framework that uses forward egocentric depth and proprioception to write a compact associative memory during forward motion and to retrieve it for collision-free backward locomotion without rearward vision. The memory backbone employs a delta-rule selective update that softly removes then writes the memory state along the active subspace. Training uses hardware-efficient parallel computation, and deployment runs recurrent, constant-time per-step inference with a constant-size state, making the approach suitable for onboard processors on low-cost robots. Experiments in both simulations and real-world scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, improving backward agility across complex terrains under limited sensing.

AI.tldr: LF2WB is a terrain-memory framework that uses forward egocentric depth and proprioception to enable collision-free backward locomotion for legged robots without rearward vision, employing delta-rule selective updates for efficient onboard deployment.
AI.motivation: Legged robots with forward-facing cameras achieve robust forward agility but cannot effectively walk backward due to lack of preview. Purely proprioceptive controllers are stable on moderate ground but fail on complex terrain and must collide with obstacles.
AI.method: The framework uses forward egocentric depth and proprioception to write a compact associative memory during forward motion, which is retrieved for backward locomotion. It employs a delta-rule selective update that softly removes and writes memory states along the active subspace. Training uses hardware-efficient parallel computation, and deployment runs recurrent, constant-time per-step inference with constant-size state.
AI.result: Experiments in both simulations and real-world scenarios demonstrate effectiveness, improving backward agility across complex terrains under limited sensing.
AI.conclusion: The approach is suitable for onboard processors on low-cost robots as it runs recurrent, constant-time per-step inference with constant-size state, enabling efficient deployment.

title: From Language to Action: Can LLM-Based Agents Be Used for Embodied Robot Cognition?
comment: Accepted for publication at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)
summary: In order to flexibly act in an everyday environment, a robotic agent needs a variety of cognitive capabilities that enable it to reason about plans and perform execution recovery. Large language models (LLMs) have been shown to demonstrate emergent cognitive aspects, such as reasoning and language understanding; however, the ability to control embodied robotic agents requires reliably bridging high-level language to low-level functionalities for perception and control. In this paper, we investigate the extent to which an LLM can serve as a core component for planning and execution reasoning in a cognitive robot architecture. For this purpose, we propose a cognitive architecture in which an agentic LLM serves as the core component for planning and reasoning, while components for working and episodic memories support learning from experience and adaptation. An instance of the architecture is then used to control a mobile manipulator in a simulated household environment, where environment interaction is done through a set of high-level tools for perception, reasoning, navigation, grasping, and placement, all of which are made available to the LLM-based agent. We evaluate our proposed system on two household tasks (object placement and object swapping), which evaluate the agent's reasoning, planning, and memory utilisation. The results demonstrate that the LLM-driven agent can complete structured tasks and exhibits emergent adaptation and memory-guided planning, but also reveal significant limitations, such as hallucinations about the task success and poor instruction following by refusing to acknowledge and complete sequential tasks. These findings highlight both the potential and challenges of employing LLMs as embodied cognitive controllers for autonomous robots.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a cognitive architecture for robots where an agentic Large Language Model (LLM) serves as the core planning and reasoning component, supported by working and episodic memories, and evaluates its performance on household tasks using a mobile manipulator in simulation.
AI.motivation: Robotic agents require cognitive capabilities for flexible action in everyday environments, including plan reasoning and execution recovery. While LLMs demonstrate emergent cognitive aspects like reasoning and language understanding, they struggle to reliably control embodied robots by bridging high-level language commands to low-level perception and control functionalities.
AI.method: The authors develop a cognitive architecture with an agentic LLM as the central component for planning and reasoning, augmented by working memory (current context) and episodic memory (experience-based learning). This system controls a mobile manipulator in a simulated household environment via high-level tools for perception, reasoning, navigation, grasping, and placement. The architecture is evaluated on two household tasks: object placement and object swapping.
AI.result: The LLM-driven agent successfully completed structured tasks and exhibited emergent adaptation and memory-guided planning capabilities. However, significant limitations were observed, including hallucinations about task success and poor instruction following, particularly regarding sequential tasks where the agent refused to acknowledge and complete multi-step instructions.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight both the potential and challenges of employing LLMs as embodied cognitive controllers for autonomous robots. While LLMs show promise for high-level planning and reasoning, their current limitations in hallucination, instruction following, and sequential task execution require further research to enable robust embodied AI applications.

title: Robotic Grasping and Placement Controlled by EEG-Based Hybrid Visual and Motor Imagery
summary: We present a framework that integrates EEG-based visual and motor imagery (VI/MI) with robotic control to enable real-time, intention-driven grasping and placement. Motivated by the promise of BCI-driven robotics to enhance human-robot interaction, this system bridges neural signals with physical control by deploying offline-pretrained decoders in a zero-shot manner within an online streaming pipeline. This establishes a dual-channel intent interface that translates visual intent into robotic actions, with VI identifying objects for grasping and MI determining placement poses, enabling intuitive control over both what to grasp and where to place. The system operates solely on EEG via a cue-free imagery protocol, achieving integration and online validation. Implemented on a Base robotic platform and evaluated across diverse scenarios, including occluded targets or varying participant postures, the system achieves online decoding accuracies of 40.23% (VI) and 62.59% (MI), with an end-to-end task success rate of 20.88%. These results demonstrate that high-level visual cognition can be decoded in real time and translated into executable robot commands, bridging the gap between neural signals and physical interaction, and validating the flexibility of a purely imagery-based BCI paradigm for practical human-robot collaboration.

AI.tldr: A BCI framework using EEG-based visual and motor imagery to enable real-time, intention-driven robotic grasping and placement through dual-channel intent decoding (cue-free, zero-shot decoding).
AI.motivation: To enhance human-robot interaction by bridging neural signals with physical control, enabling intuitive control over what to grasp and where to place objects through BCI-driven robotics.
AI.method: A dual-channel intent interface where visual imagery (VI) identifies objects for grasping and motor imagery (MI) determines placement poses, implemented via offline-pretrained decoders in a zero-shot manner within an online streaming pipeline using a cue-free imagery protocol on EEG signals.
AI.result: Achieved online decoding accuracies of 40.23% (VI) and 62.59% (MI) across diverse scenarios including occluded targets and varying postures, with an end-to-end task success rate of 20.88% on a Base robotic platform.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that high-level visual cognition can be decoded in real-time and translated into executable robot commands, validating the flexibility of a purely imagery-based BCI paradigm for practical human-robot collaboration.

title: ACE-Brain-0: Spatial Intelligence as a Shared Scaffold for Universal Embodiments
comment: Code: https://github.com/ACE-BRAIN-Team/ACE-Brain-0 Hugging Face: https://huggingface.co/ACE-Brain/ACE-Brain-0-8B
summary: Universal embodied intelligence demands robust generalization across heterogeneous embodiments, such as autonomous driving, robotics, and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). However, existing embodied brain in training a unified model over diverse embodiments frequently triggers long-tail data, gradient interference, and catastrophic forgetting, making it notoriously difficult to balance universal generalization with domain-specific proficiency. In this report, we introduce ACE-Brain-0, a generalist foundation brain that unifies spatial reasoning, autonomous driving, and embodied manipulation within a single multimodal large language model~(MLLM). Our key insight is that spatial intelligence serves as a universal scaffold across diverse physical embodiments: although vehicles, robots, and UAVs differ drastically in morphology, they share a common need for modeling 3D mental space, making spatial cognition a natural, domain-agnostic foundation for cross-embodiment transfer. Building on this insight, we propose the Scaffold-Specialize-Reconcile~(SSR) paradigm, which first establishes a shared spatial foundation, then cultivates domain-specialized experts, and finally harmonizes them through data-free model merging. Furthermore, we adopt Group Relative Policy Optimization~(GRPO) to strengthen the model's comprehensive capability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ACE-Brain-0 achieves competitive and even state-of-the-art performance across 24 spatial and embodiment-related benchmarks.

AI.tldr: ACE-Brain-0 is a unified multimodal large language model for embodied intelligence that uses spatial reasoning as a common foundation through the Scaffold-Specialize-Reconcile paradigm and GRPO training, achieving state-of-the-art performance across 24 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Universal embodied intelligence requires robust generalization across heterogeneous embodiments (autonomous driving, robotics, UAVs), but existing unified models face challenges including long-tail data, gradient interference, and catastrophic forgetting when balancing universal generalization with domain-specific proficiency.
AI.method: The paper introduces ACE-Brain-0, a generalist foundation brain using the Scaffold-Specialize-Reconcile (SSR) paradigm: 1) Scaffold: establishes a shared spatial foundation; 2) Specialize: cultivates domain-specialized experts; 3) Reconcile: harmonizes through data-free model merging. The model employs Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to strengthen comprehensive capabilities.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that ACE-Brain-0 achieves competitive and even state-of-the-art performance across 24 spatial and embodiment-related benchmarks, validating the effectiveness of spatial intelligence as a universal scaffold for cross-embodiment transfer.
AI.conclusion: Spatial cognition serves as a natural, domain-agnostic foundation for cross-embodiment transfer, and the SSR paradigm with data-free model merging provides an effective approach to balancing universal generalization with domain-specific proficiency in embodied AI systems.

title: HoMMI: Learning Whole-Body Mobile Manipulation from Human Demonstrations
summary: We present Whole-Body Mobile Manipulation Interface (HoMMI), a data collection and policy learning framework that learns whole-body mobile manipulation directly from robot-free human demonstrations. We augment UMI interfaces with egocentric sensing to capture the global context required for mobile manipulation, enabling portable, robot-free, and scalable data collection. However, naively incorporating egocentric sensing introduces a larger human-to-robot embodiment gap in both observation and action spaces, making policy transfer difficult. We explicitly bridge this gap with a cross-embodiment hand-eye policy design, including an embodiment agnostic visual representation; a relaxed head action representation; and a whole-body controller that realizes hand-eye trajectories through coordinated whole-body motion under robot-specific physical constraints. Together, these enable long-horizon mobile manipulation tasks requiring bimanual and whole-body coordination, navigation, and active perception. Results are best viewed on: https://hommi-robot.github.io

AI.tldr: HoMMI is a robot-free data collection and policy learning framework for whole-body mobile manipulation that bridges the human-to-robot embodiment gap through cross-embodiment policy design with embodiment-agnostic visual representation, relaxed head actions, and a whole-body controller.
AI.motivation: The authors identify a need for scalable, robot-free data collection for mobile manipulation tasks. They note that while UMI interfaces enable data collection, they lack the global context required for mobile manipulation. Naively incorporating egocentric sensing creates a larger human-to-robot embodiment gap in both observation and action spaces, making policy transfer difficult.
AI.method: The framework augments UMI interfaces with egocentric sensing for global context. It addresses the embodiment gap through three key components: 1) Embodiment-agnostic visual representation, 2) Relaxed head action representation, and 3) Whole-body controller that realizes hand-eye trajectories through coordinated motion under robot-specific constraints. This enables policy learning from human demonstrations for complex long-horizon tasks.
AI.result: The framework enables long-horizon mobile manipulation tasks requiring bimanual coordination, whole-body motion, navigation, and active perception. The results demonstrate successful policy transfer from human demonstrations to robots through the cross-embodiment design.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly bridging the human-to-robot embodiment gap through cross-embodiment policy design, HoMMI achieves scalable robot-free data collection and effective policy learning for complex whole-body mobile manipulation tasks that require coordinated hand-eye control and navigation.

title: Tether: Autonomous Functional Play with Correspondence-Driven Trajectory Warping
comment: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2026. Project website and code: https://tether-research.github.io
summary: The ability to conduct and learn from interaction and experience is a central challenge in robotics, offering a scalable alternative to labor-intensive human demonstrations. However, realizing such "play" requires (1) a policy robust to diverse, potentially out-of-distribution environment states, and (2) a procedure that continuously produces useful robot experience. To address these challenges, we introduce Tether, a method for autonomous functional play involving structured, task-directed interactions. First, we design a novel open-loop policy that warps actions from a small set of source demonstrations (<=10) by anchoring them to semantic keypoint correspondences in the target scene. We show that this design is extremely data-efficient and robust even under significant spatial and semantic variations. Second, we deploy this policy for autonomous functional play in the real world via a continuous cycle of task selection, execution, evaluation, and improvement, guided by the visual understanding capabilities of vision-language models. This procedure generates diverse, high-quality datasets with minimal human intervention. In a household-like multi-object setup, our method is the first to perform many hours of autonomous multi-task play in the real world starting from only a handful of demonstrations. This produces a stream of data that consistently improves the performance of closed-loop imitation policies over time, ultimately yielding over 1000 expert-level trajectories and training policies competitive with those learned from human-collected demonstrations.

AI.tldr: Tether is a method for autonomous functional play that uses a novel open-loop policy with semantic keypoint correspondences and a continuous improvement cycle guided by vision-language models to generate high-quality datasets for training robot policies.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses two core challenges in robotics: (1) designing policies robust to diverse, potentially out-of-distribution environment states, and (2) creating procedures that continuously produce useful robot experience. The goal is to enable "play" as a scalable alternative to labor-intensive human demonstrations.
AI.method: 1. Design a novel open-loop policy that warps actions from ≤10 source demonstrations by anchoring them to semantic keypoint correspondences in the target scene. 2. Deploy for autonomous functional play via a continuous cycle of task selection, execution, evaluation, and improvement, guided by vision-language model visual understanding capabilities.
AI.result: The method generates diverse, high-quality datasets with minimal human intervention, performs many hours of autonomous multi-task play in a real-world household-like multi-object setup starting from only a handful of demonstrations, and produces over 1000 expert-level trajectories that consistently improve closed-loop imitation policies, ultimately yielding policies competitive with those learned from human demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: Tether demonstrates that autonomous functional play with semantic keypoint-based policies and continuous improvement cycles is a viable approach for scalable robot learning, enabling the generation of high-quality data to train expert-level robot policies with minimal human intervention.

title: ULTRA: Unified Multimodal Control for Autonomous Humanoid Whole-Body Loco-Manipulation
comment: Project Page: https://ultra-humanoid.github.io/
summary: Achieving autonomous and versatile whole-body loco-manipulation remains a central barrier to making humanoids practically useful. Yet existing approaches are fundamentally constrained: retargeted data are often scarce or low-quality; methods struggle to scale to large skill repertoires; and, most importantly, they rely on tracking predefined motion references rather than generating behavior from perception and high-level task specifications. To address these limitations, we propose ULTRA, a unified framework with two key components. First, we introduce a physics-driven neural retargeting algorithm that translates large-scale motion capture to humanoid embodiments while preserving physical plausibility for contact-rich interactions. Second, we learn a unified multimodal controller that supports both dense references and sparse task specifications, under sensing ranging from accurate motion-capture state to noisy egocentric visual inputs. We distill a universal tracking policy into this controller, compress motor skills into a compact latent space, and apply reinforcement learning finetuning to expand coverage and improve robustness under out-of-distribution scenarios. This enables coordinated whole-body behavior from sparse intent without test-time reference motions. We evaluate ULTRA in simulation and on a real Unitree G1 humanoid. Results show that ULTRA generalizes to autonomous, goal-conditioned whole-body loco-manipulation from egocentric perception, consistently outperforming tracking-only baselines with limited skills.

AI.tldr: ULTRA is a unified framework for humanoid whole-body loco-manipulation that combines physics-driven neural retargeting with a multimodal controller, enabling autonomous behavior generation from sparse task specifications without test-time reference motions.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches are limited by scarce/low-quality retargeted data, inability to scale to large skill repertoires, and reliance on tracking predefined motion references rather than generating behavior from perception and task specifications.
AI.method: Two key components: (1) Physics-driven neural retargeting algorithm that translates large-scale motion capture to humanoid embodiments while preserving physical plausibility for contact-rich interactions; (2) Unified multimodal controller supporting dense references and sparse task specifications from various sensing inputs, which distills a universal tracking policy, compresses motor skills into a compact latent space, and applies reinforcement learning finetuning for improved robustness.
AI.result: ULTRA generalizes to autonomous, goal-conditioned whole-body loco-manipulation from egocentric perception, consistently outperforming tracking-only baselines with limited skills in both simulation and on a real Unitree G1 humanoid.
AI.conclusion: ULTRA successfully addresses the core limitations of existing approaches by enabling coordinated whole-body behavior from sparse intent without requiring test-time reference motions, demonstrating that unified learning frameworks can achieve both dense tracking and sparse task-driven control for humanoid manipulation.

title: How to Peel with a Knife: Aligning Fine-Grained Manipulation with Human Preference
comment: Project page can be found at https://toruowo.github.io/peel
summary: Many essential manipulation tasks - such as food preparation, surgery, and craftsmanship - remain intractable for autonomous robots. These tasks are characterized not only by contact-rich, force-sensitive dynamics, but also by their "implicit" success criteria: unlike pick-and-place, task quality in these domains is continuous and subjective (e.g. how well a potato is peeled), making quantitative evaluation and reward engineering difficult. We present a learning framework for such tasks, using peeling with a knife as a representative example. Our approach follows a two-stage pipeline: first, we learn a robust initial policy via force-aware data collection and imitation learning, enabling generalization across object variations; second, we refine the policy through preference-based finetuning using a learned reward model that combines quantitative task metrics with qualitative human feedback, aligning policy behavior with human notions of task quality. Using only 50-200 peeling trajectories, our system achieves over 90% average success rates on challenging produce including cucumbers, apples, and potatoes, with performance improving by up to 40% through preference-based finetuning. Remarkably, policies trained on a single produce category exhibit strong zero-shot generalization to unseen in-category instances and to out-of-distribution produce from different categories while maintaining over 90% success rates.

AI.tldr: A two-stage learning framework for force-sensitive manipulation tasks with implicit success criteria, using peeling as a representative example. It combines force-aware imitation learning with preference-based fine-tuning using a learned reward model that integrates quantitative metrics and qualitative human feedback.
AI.motivation: Essential manipulation tasks like food preparation, surgery, and craftsmanship are intractable for autonomous robots due to their contact-rich, force-sensitive dynamics and "implicit" success criteria (continuous, subjective evaluation) that make quantitative assessment and reward engineering difficult.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) Force-aware data collection and imitation learning to learn a robust initial policy that generalizes across object variations; (2) Preference-based fine-tuning using a learned reward model that combines quantitative task metrics with qualitative human feedback to align policy behavior with human notions of task quality.
AI.result: With only 50-200 peeling trajectories, achieves over 90% average success rates on cucumbers, apples, and potatoes. Performance improves by up to 40% through preference-based fine-tuning. Policies trained on single categories exhibit strong zero-shot generalization to unseen in-category instances and out-of-distribution produce from different categories.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully enables learning of tasks with implicit success criteria, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities that maintain high performance across different object categories and unseen instances.
